

===== PAGE 1 =====

Learning  Interpretable  Feature  Spaces
 via  Domain-Informed  Encoders  
MSc   Dissertation  —  Module  CS4700A  
  Submitted  by:  
Bhoopesh  Nandan  Singh    
Student
 
Id:
 
250142934
 Academic  Year  2025–2026   
Supervisor  :  Dr.  Roberto  Alamino  
   This  dissertation  is  submitted  in  partial  fulfillment  of  the  requirements  for  the  award  of  MSc  
Artificial
 
Intelligence
 
at
 
Aston
 
University.
 
 


===== PAGE 2 =====
       
Declaration  
I  declare  that  I  have  personally  prepared  this  assignment.  The  work  is  my  own,  carried  out  
personally
 
by
 
me
 
unless
 
otherwise
 
stated
 
and
 
has
 
not
 
been
 
generated
 
using
 
paid
 
for
 
assessment
 
writing
 
services
 
or
 
Artificial
 
Intelligence
 
tools
 
unless
 
specified
 
as
 
a
 
clearly
 
stated
 
approved
 
component
 
of
 
the
 
assessment
 
brief.
 
All
 
sources
 
of
 
information,
 
including
 
quotations,
 
are
 
acknowledged
 
by
 
means
 
of
 
the
 
appropriate
 
citations
 
and
 
references.
 
I
 
declare
 
that
 
this
 
work
 
has
 
not
 
gained
 
credit
 
previously
 
for
 
another
 
module
 
at
 
this
 
or
 
another
 
University,
 
save
 
for
 
permitted
 
elements
 
which
 
formed
 
part
 
of
 
an
 
associated
 
proposal
 
linked
 
directly
 
to
 
this
 
submission.I
 
understand
 
that
 
plagiarism,
 
collusion,
 
copying
 
another
 
student
 
and
 
commissioning
 
(which
 
for
 
the
 
avoidance
 
of
 
doubt
 
includes
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
essay
 
mills
 
and
 
other
 
paid
 
for
 
assessment
 
writing
 
services,
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
unattributed
 
use
 
of
 
work
 
generated
 
by
 
Artificial
 
Intelligence
 
tools)
 
are
 
regarded
 
as
 
offences
 
against
 
the
 
University’s
 
Assessment
 
Regulations
 
and
 
may
 
result
 
in
 
formal
 
disciplinary
 
proceedings.I
 
understand
 
that
 
by
 
submitting
 
this
 
assessment,
 
I
 
declare
 
myself
 
fit
 
to
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
undertake
 
the
 
assessment
 
and
 
accept
 
the
 
outcome
 
of
 
the
 
assessment
 
as
 
valid.
 
 Student  Signature:   Bhoopesh  Nandan  Singh  
Date:  12-April-2026  
Academic  Year  2025  –  2026  
 


===== PAGE 3 =====
       
Abstract  
In  most  concept-driven  interpretability  approaches,  the  process  starts  from  an  already  defined  
set.
 
The
 
expert
 
or
 
scientist
 
decides
 
beforehand
 
on
 
which
 
concepts
 
are
 
important
 
and
 
marks
 
examples
 
of
 
such
 
concepts.
 
However,
 
this
 
is
 
not
 
possible
 
when
 
interpreting
 
data
 
in
 
the
 
clinic,
 
since
 
the
 
concepts
 
needed
 
are
 
the
 
ones
 
to
 
be
 
uncovered.
 
This  dissertation  introduces  the  Domain-Informed  Feature  Encoder  (DIFE)  pipeline,  which  
contradicts
 
this
 
assumption
 
by
 
turning
 
it
 
around.
 
This
 
approach
 
extends
 
DINO
 
Vision
 
Transformers
 
for
 
image
 
data
 
and
 
employs
 
our
 
own
 
PanelFTTransformer
 
for
 
clinical
 
blood
 
panels.
 
Potential
 
concepts
 
are
 
detected
 
using
 
Gaussian
 
Mixture
 
Model
 
clustering,
 
followed
 
by
 
assigning
 
names
 
to
 
them
 
using
 
a
 
human
 
evaluator
 
or
 
LLM
 
in
 
charge
 
of
 
the
 
task
 
–
 
labeling
 
happens
 
post-training,
 
and
 
no
 
concept
 
labels
 
are
 
utilized
 
throughout
 
the
 
whole
 
process
 
of
 
training.
 
As  for  the  performance  in  the  image  domain,  it  greatly  surpassed  all  expectations.  Multi-level  
feature
 
extraction
 
from
 
blocks
 
8,
 
10,
 
and
 
12
 
provided
 
a
 
75%
 
increase
 
in
 
silhouette
 
scores
 
compared
 
to
 
single-level
 
feature
 
extraction.
 
Moreover,
 
a
 
concept-based
 
classifier
 
reached
 
98.9%
 
accuracy
 
on
 
the
 
test
 
dataset
 
and
 
scored
 
a
 
macro
 
F1
 
of
 
0.99
 
–
 
this
 
result
 
significantly
 
exceeds
 
the
 
accuracy
 
obtained
 
using
 
the
 
raw
 
feature
 
baseline.
 
As
 
for
 
the
 
clinical
 
tabular
 
data
 
domain,
 
patterns
 
corresponding
 
to
 
dyslipidemia,
 
metabolic
 
syndrome,
 
and
 
kidney
 
damage
 
were
 
extracted
 
from
 
the
 
data
 
containing
 
information
 
about
 
18,673
 
patients.
 
A
 
classifier
 
based
 
on
 
three
 
classes
 
reached
 
50.9%
 
accuracy,
 
which
 
is
 
considerably
 
higher
 
than
 
33.3%,
 
which
 
is
 
the
 
baseline
 
of
 
a
 
random
 
classifier.
 
One  consistent  trend  emerged  and  proved  itself  again:  the  interpretable  classifier  
outperformed
 
the
 
baseline.
 
To
 
be
 
exact,
 
contrary
 
to
 
previous
 
expectations,
 
there
 
were
 
no
 
trade-offs
 
with
 
regards
 
to
 
the
 
accuracy.
 
Keywords:  interpretable  machine  learning,  self-supervised  learning,  Vision  Transformer,  
DINO,
 
FT-Transformer,
 
Gaussian
 
Mixture
 
Model,
 
concept
 
activation
 
vectors,
 
unsupervised
 
concept
 
discovery,
 
panel
 
positional
 
encoding,
 
clinical
 
AI
 
 


===== PAGE 4 =====

Literature  Review  and  Contextual  Analysis ....................................................... 11 2.1  Concept-Based  Interpretability  -  Gap  1 ........................................................... 11 2.2  Self-Supervised  Visual  Encoders  -  Gap  2  (Image  Domain) ............................ 12 2.3  Tabular  Deep  Learning  and  Panel  Structure  -  Gap  2  (Tabular  Domain) ......... 12 2.4  LLM-Mediated  Concept  Validation  —  Gap  3 ................................................... 13 2.5  Domain  Knowledge  Integration  Taxonomy ..................................................... 14 2.6  Critical  Gap  Analysis ....................................................................................... 14 3.  Problem  Description  and  Requirements ............................................................ 16 3.1  Problem  Statement ......................................................................................... 16 3.2  Functional  Requirements ................................................................................ 16 3.3  Non-Functional  Requirements ........................................................................ 17 4.  Practical  Realisation:  Design,  Implementation  and  Testing ............................. 18 4.1  Shared  Architecture  Overview ........................................................................ 18 4.2  Image  Domain  —  DINO  Pipeline .................................................................... 19 4.2.1  Dataset  and  Preprocessing .................................................................... 19 4.2.2  Multi-Layer  Feature  Extraction  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Architecture) ............. 19 4.2.3  Attention-Guided  Foreground  Masking  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  1,  Architecture) 19 4.2.4  GMM  Concept  Clustering  (Pillar  1  —  Gap  1) .......................................... 20 4.2.5  Semantic  Consistency  Fine-Tuning  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Loss) ................. 20 4.2.6  Concept  Labelling  and  Activation  Scoring  (Pillar  3  —  Gap  1) ................ 20 4.3  Tabular  Domain  —  PanelFTTransformer  (PSFTT)  Pipeline ...................... 21 4.3.1  Dataset ................................................................................................... 21 4.3.2  Clinical  Deviation  Encoding  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Input) ............................ 21 4.3.3  PanelFTTransformer  Architecture  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Architecture) ....... 21 4.3.4  Two-Scale  SSL  Pre-Training  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Loss) ........................... 22 4.3.5  LLM  Clinical  Concept  Labelling  (Pillar  3  —  Gap  3) ................................. 22 4.4  Shared  Downstream  Components .................................................................. 23 4.4.1  Development  Environment ..................................................................... 23 4.4.2  Software  Engineering  Practices .............................................................. 23 4.5  Testing  and  Experimental  Validation ............................................................... 24 


===== PAGE 5 =====

Project  Management  and  Methodology .............................................................. 26 5.1  Research  Methodology ................................................................................... 26 5.2  Development  Process  and  Sprint  Plan ........................................................... 26 5.3  Risk  Assessment  and  Mitigation ..................................................................... 27 5.4  Deviations  from  Original  Plan .......................................................................... 28 6.  Evaluation,  Reflection  and  Ethical  Implications ................................................ 29 6.1  Experimental  Results  and  Analysis ................................................................. 29 6.1.1  Multi-Layer  Ablation  (Gap  2  Evidence) ................................................... 29 6.1.2  Classification  Performance  (Gap  1  Evidence) ........................................ 31 6.1.3  Semantic  Accuracy  Evaluation  (Gap  1  +  Gap  3  Evidence) .................... 31 6.1.4  Tabular  Domain  —  Clinical  Concept  Discovery ...................................... 32 6.2  Objectives  vs.  Outcomes ................................................................................ 33 6.3  Comparison  with  Alternative  Approaches ....................................................... 33 6.4  Critical  Appraisal  —  What  Worked  and  What  Did  Not .................................... 34 6.5  Ethical,  Legal  and  Commercial  Implications ................................................... 35 6.6  Personal  Reflection ......................................................................................... 35 7. ===== PAGE 6 =====
       
1.  Introduction  
1.1  Motivation  
Models  like  Vision  Transformers  are  good  at  making  predictions  but  poor  at  explaining  how  
they
 
were
 
made.
 
VT
 
models
 
can
 
classify
 
chest
 
X-rays
 
to
 
radiologist
 
accuracy
 
level,
 
while
 
FT-Transformers
 
can
 
detect
 
diabetic
 
risk
 
in
 
patients'
 
blood
 
panels
 
with
 
reasonable
 
precision.
 
Once
 
asked
 
to
 
explain
 
why
 
exactly
 
they
 
reached
 
their
 
conclusions,
 
they
 
produce
 
a
 
384-dimensional
 
vector
 
with
 
little
 
relevance
 
to
 
anything
 
that
 
could
 
be
 
clinically
 
meaningful
 
to
 
humans.
 
This
 
gap
 
between
 
prediction
 
capability
 
and
 
explainability
 
is
 
what
 
this
 
dissertation
 
tackles.
 
While
 
post
 
hoc
 
explanation
 
techniques,
 
such
 
as
 
GradCAM,
 
may
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
highlight
 
which
 
pixels
 
are
 
important
 
to
 
the
 
final
 
decision,
 
attributions
 
and
 
concept-level
 
explanations
 
are
 
not
 
the
 
same
 
thing.
 
Even
 
if
 
there
 
is
 
some
 
alignment
 
between
 
attribution
 
maps
 
and
 
the
 
actual
 
lesion's
 
border,
 
this
 
doesn't
 
mean
 
that
 
the
 
model
 
has
 
considered
 
lesion
 
boundary
 
irregularity
 
in
 
making
 
a
 
decision,
 
something
 
that
 
could
 
be
 
clinically
 
useful,
 
whereas
 
pixel
 
attributions
 
imply
 
extra
 
mental
 
effort
 
on
 
the
 
part
 
of
 
the
 
practitioner.
 
Concept-based
 
methods
 
like
 
TCAV
 
(Kim
 
et
 
al.,
 
2018)
 
and
 
Concept
 
Bottleneck
 
Models
 
(CBM;
 
Koh
 
et
 
al.,
 
2020)
 
attempt
 
to
 
bridge
 
the
 
gap
 
between
 
machine
 
and
 
man
 
through
 
meaningful
 
concept
 
extraction.
 
Yet
 
they
 
too
 
rely
 
on
 
predetermined
 
concept
 
labels
 
before
 
training,
 
a
 
necessary
 
prerequisite
 
that
 
may
 
not
 
exist
 
in
 
real-world
 
applications,
 
particularly
 
clinical
 
ones.
 
The
 
proposed
 
solution
 
embeds
 
some
 
information
 
about
 
domain
 
structure
 
(panel
 
composition
 
in
 
the
 
case
 
of
 
tabular
 
data,
 
spatial
 
parts
 
in
 
the
 
case
 
of
 
vision
 
tasks)
 
into
 
the
 
architecture
 
of
 
the
 
encoder
 
and
 
then
 
extracts
 
unlabeled
 
concepts
 
based
 
on
 
features.
 
Provided
 
the
 
architecture
 
is
 
sound,
 
latent-space
 
clusters
 
will
 
represent
 
concepts
 
as
 
would
 
be
 
identified
 
by
 
domain
 
experts.
 
Experiments
 
included
 
in
 
this
 
dissertation
 
demonstrate
 
that
 
this
 
is
 
true
 
at
 
least
 
in
 
the
 
two
 
domains
 
analyzed.
 
1.2  The  Three  Literature  Gaps  
There  are  at  least  three  limitations  found  in  the  literature  reviewed  to  motivate  the  research  
conducted
 
in
 
this
 
thesis.
 
The
 
aforementioned
 
limitations
 
will
 
be
 
elaborated
 
in
 
Chapter
 
2
 
below.
 
Gap  1  –  the  need  to  know  the  concepts  in  advance.  TCAV  and  CBMs  require  some  concept  
samples
 
before
 
training,
 
even
 
though
 
ACE
 
(Ghorbani
 
et
 
al.,
 
2019)
 
automates
 
the
 
candidate
 
generation
 
process.
 
Hence,
 
these
 
methods
 
are
 
confined
 
to
 
only
 
certain
 
domains
 
where
 
the
 
relevant
 
concepts
 
have
 
already
 
been
 
determined.
 


===== PAGE 7 =====
       Gap  2  –  underexploration  of  intermediate  transformer  layers.  According  to  Caron  et  al.  
(2021),
 
the
 
architecture
 
of
 
the
 
intermediate
 
layers
 
of
 
the
 
DINO
 
framework
 
has
 
been
 
intentionally
 
overlooked,
 
a
 
conclusion
 
echoed
 
for
 
tabular
 
transformers
 
in
 
Gorishniy
 
et
 
al.
 
(2021).
 
Thus,
 
intermediate
 
representations
 
are
 
treated
 
as
 
mere
 
implementation
 
details.
 
Gap  3  –  lack  of  an  LLM-driven  concept  naming  mechanism  for  clinical  numerical  data.  
ClusterLLM
 
(Zhang
 
et
 
al.,
 
2023)
 
proves
 
that
 
it
 
is
 
possible
 
for
 
an
 
LLM
 
to
 
help
 
name
 
clusters
 
discovered
 
in
 
the
 
domain
 
of
 
natural
 
language
 
processing.
 
Yet
 
no
 
method
 
has
 
ever
 
applied
 
an
 
LLM
 
for
 
concept
 
naming
 
in
 
the
 
numerical
 
clinical
 
domain,
 
let
 
alone
 
verifying
 
its
 
clinical
 
sense.
 
1.3  The  Three  Design  Pillars  
The  first  design  choice  follows  from  Gap  1.  Namely,  concepts  can  be  discovered  using  a  
Gaussian
 
Mixture
 
Model
 
clustering
 
of
 
feature
 
vectors
 
generated
 
by
 
the
 
encoder.
 
Labelling,
 
whether
 
performed
 
by
 
a
 
human
 
or
 
the
 
LLM,
 
occurs
 
after
 
concept
 
discovery,
 
not
 
before.
 
The  second  design  choice  addresses  Gap  2:  features  should  be  extracted  from  multiple  layers  
of
 
intermediate
 
transformer
 
blocks
 
and
 
concatenated.
 
For
 
DINO
 
this
 
means
 
layers
 
8,
 
10,
 
and
 
12;
 
for
 
PSFTT,
 
this
 
implies
 
layers
 
L2,
 
L4,
 
and
 
L6.
 
Additionally,
 
a
 
foreground
 
mask
 
guided
 
by
 
the
 
attention
 
maps
 
is
 
used
 
for
 
image
 
features.
 
For
 
tabular
 
clinical
 
features,
 
an
 
additional
 
source
 
of
 
domain
 
structure
 
information
 
is
 
provided
 
through
 
panel
 
positional
 
encoding
 
and
 
two-scale
 
supervised
 
self-learning.
 
Based
 
on
 
the
 
taxonomy
 
suggested
 
by
 
Von
 
Rueden
 
et
 
al.
 
(2021),
 
the
 
design
 
principles
 
are
 
consistent
 
with
 
the
 
following
 
three
 
categories
 
of
 
domain
 
knowledge
 
embedding:
 
architectural
 
embedding
 
(multiple
 
layer
 
extraction,
 
foreground
 
masking,
 
panel
 
position
 
embedding),
 
loss-based
 
objectives
 
(fine-tuning
 
semantic
 
consistency,
 
two-level
 
self-supervised
 
learning),
 
and
 
input
 
embedding
 
(clinical
 
deviation
 
normalization).
 
The  third  design  choice  arises  from  Gap  3:  the  discovered  concepts  should  receive  semantic  
labels
 
after
 
concept
 
discovery.
 
Image
 
clusters
 
receive
 
labels
 
using
 
a
 
Streamlit
 
interface
 
showing
 
sample
 
patches.
 
Tabular
 
clusters
 
receive
 
labels
 
using
 
an
 
LLM
 
Clinical
 
Advisor
 
assessing
 
a
 
patient
 
profile
 
deviation
 
and
 
providing
 
a
 
structured
 
clinical
 
diagnosis,
 
which
 
is
 
then
 
evaluated
 
and,
 
if
 
needed,
 
corrected
 
by
 
a
 
human.
 
For  each  design  principle,  an  ablation  experiment  is  performed  to  establish  its  role.  The  
cause-and-effect
 
relationship
 
between
 
the
 
gap,
 
design
 
decision,
 
and
 
experiment
 
forms
 
the
 
basis
 
of
 
the
 
result
 
presentation
 
structure. 


===== PAGE 8 =====
       1.4  Two-Domain  Validation  
The  methodology  is  validated  in  two  domains  –  visual  (image  data)  and  clinical  (tabular  data).  
The
 
former
 
is
 
chosen
 
to
 
enable
 
visual
 
inspection
 
of
 
concept
 
quality,
 
while
 
the
 
latter
 
represents
 
the
 
real-life
 
clinical
 
application
 
scenario
 
for
 
which
 
the
 
pipeline
 
was
 
conceived.
 
The
 
same
 
downstream
 
process
 
of
 
clustering,
 
labelling,
 
concept
 
evaluation,
 
and
 
classification
 
is
 
implemented
 
in
 
both
 
domains
 
without
 
domain-specific
 
stages
 
of
 
implementation
 
to
 
prove
 
domain
 
generality
 
(NFR5).
 
A  visual  dataset  allows  a  proof-of-concept  demonstration,  where  the  quality  of  discovered  
concepts
 
is
 
evident
 
through
 
human
 
inspection
 
of
 
how
 
well
 
a
 
named
 
concept
 
matches
 
its
 
associated
 
visual
 
patches—for
 
instance,
 
inspecting
 
whether
 
“cat
 
ear”
 
indeed
 
looks
 
like
 
the
 
part
 
of
 
an
 
image
 
associated
 
with
 
that
 
name.
 
This
 
is
 
impossible
 
in
 
the
 
case
 
of
 
a
 
medical
 
tabular
 
dataset,
 
because
 
in
 
the
 
latter
 
the
 
meaning
 
of
 
a
 
cluster
 
cannot
 
be
 
understood
 
visually
 
but
 
only
 
by
 
analyzing
 
its
 
deviation
 
profile.
 
By
 
starting
 
with
 
a
 
proof-of-concept
 
in
 
the
 
image
 
domain,
 
one
 
establishes
 
a
 
verifiable
 
benchmark
 
before
 
extending
 
the
 
architecture
 
into
 
the
 
more
 
challenging
 
clinical
 
setting.
 
On  the  other  hand,  the  tabular  domain,  based  on  NHANES  blood  panel  data,  provides  the  
second,
 
co-equal
 
role:
 
it
 
checks
 
whether
 
the
 
concept
 
discovery
 
framework
 
is
 
indeed
 
generally
 
applicable
 
to
 
an
 
entirely
 
different
 
data
 
modality—one
 
which
 
lacks
 
any
 
spatial
 
structure,
 
features
 
heterogeneous
 
scale,
 
and
 
is
 
characterized
 
by
 
explicitly
 
clinically
 
defined
 
normal
 
ranges.
 
Should
 
this
 
framework
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
yield
 
only
 
meaningful
 
concepts
 
in
 
the
 
image
 
domain,
 
the
 
proposed
 
solution
 
would
 
not
 
go
 
beyond
 
computer
 
vision
 
applications.
 
The
 
fact
 
that
 
it
 
can
 
produce
 
meaningful
 
clinical
 
concepts
 
without
 
any
 
outcome
 
labels
 
marks
 
this
 
work
 
as
 
a
 
domain-general
 
interpretability
 
framework.
 
Taken  together,  both  domains  make  up  an  ideal  validation  pair:  a  human-verifiable  semantic  
validation
 
for
 
images
 
and
 
a
 
clinically
 
meaningful
 
validation
 
for
 
tables.
 
In
 
no
 
previously
 
explored
 
concept-discovery
 
framework
 
discussed
 
in
 
Chapter
 
2
 
did
 
these
 
two
 
aspects
 
coincide.
 
1.5  Aim  and  Objectives  
Aim:  Design,  implement,  and  evaluate  a  pipeline  capable  of  discovering  interpretable  
concepts
 
from
 
unlabeled
 
data,
 
labeling
 
them
 
semantically
 
using
 
domain
 
knowledge,
 
and
 
enabling
 
predictions
 
grounded
 
in
 
the
 
named
 
concepts
 
using
 
image
 
and
 
clinical
 
tabular
 
data.
 
 


===== PAGE 9 =====
       Objectives:  
Objective  1.  Create  a  pipeline  for  concept  discovery  involving  foreground  masking,  spatial  
data
 
augmentation,
 
and
 
semantic
 
fine-tuning
 
of
 
the
 
DINO
 
ViT-S/8
 
model,
 
with
 
the
 
caveat
 
that
 
there
 
will
 
be
 
no
 
labeled
 
data
 
involved
 
in
 
any
 
step
 
of
 
the
 
process.
 
Deliverable:
 
Python
 
code
 
repository
 
with
 
the
 
implementation
 
on
 
GitHub.
 
Due
 
date:
 
End
 
of
 
Sprint
 
3.
 
Objective  2.  Perform  ablation  studies  comparing  different  approaches  to  feature  extraction  
and
 
analyze
 
their
 
performance
 
using
 
metrics
 
such
 
as
 
silhouette
 
score,
 
normalized
 
mutual
 
information
 
(NMI),
 
class
 
purity,
 
and
 
AUC-ROC.
 
Deliverable:
 
Table
 
with
 
the
 
results
 
of
 
ablation
 
studies.
 
Due
 
date:
 
End
 
of
 
Sprint
 
5.
 
Objective  3.  Develop  a  pipeline  for  the  parallel  analysis  of  NHANES  blood  panel  data  using  
PanelFTTransformer
 
encoder
 
and
 
concept
 
labeling
 
driven
 
by
 
large
 
language
 
models.
 
Deliverable:
 
Results
 
obtained
 
through
 
the
 
labeling
 
and
 
scoring
 
pipelines
 
along
 
with
 
clinical
 
concept
 
table.
 
Due
 
date:
 
End
 
of
 
Sprint
 
6.
 
Objective  4.  Confirm  that  the  performance  of  the  concept-based  classifier  is  comparable  to  
that
 
of
 
the
 
feature-based
 
classifier.
 
Deliverable:
 
Table
 
summarizing
 
the
 
classification
 
results.
 
Due
 
date:
 
End
 
of
 
Sprint
 
7.
 
1.6  Legal,  Ethical  and  Professional  Context  
Both  datasets  are  publicly  available  and  anonymized.  STL-10  can  be  freely  used  for  academic  
purposes
 
(Coates
 
et
 
al.,
 
2011).
 
NHANES
 
datasets
 
are
 
published
 
by
 
the
 
CDC
 
and
 
all
 
the
 
identifying
 
variables
 
are
 
stripped.
 
Annotations
 
for
 
Oxford-IIIT
 
pet
 
mask
 
(Parkhi
 
et
 
al.,
 
2012)
 
are
 
only
 
used
 
on
 
100-image
 
validation
 
set
 
to
 
measure
 
foreground
 
mask
 
quality,
 
not
 
for
 
training.
 
Weights
 
of
 
the
 
DINO
 
model
 
are
 
provided
 
under
 
the
 
Apache
 
2.0
 
license.
 
Labeling
 
tools
 
operate
 
exclusively
 
in
 
local
 
mode
 
and
 
do
 
not
 
transmit
 
data
 
anywhere.
 
The
 
clinical
 
application
 
motivation
 
aligns
 
with
 
GDPR
 
Article
 
22
 
and
 
EU
 
AI
 
Act.
 
The
 
explanation
 
format
 
("Prediction:
 
Metabolic
 
syndrome
 
(87%
 
confidence);
 
driven
 
by
 
elevated
 
glucose
 
pattern
 
(0.71)
 
and
 
borderline
 
lipid
 
pattern
 
(0.43)")
 
complies
 
with
 
the
 
essence
 
of
 
the
 
Right
 
to
 
Explanation.
 
The
 
human-in-the-loop
 
component
 
of
 
the
 
process
 
conforms
 
to
 
the
 
EU
 
AI
 
Act
 
high-risk
 
category.
 
 


===== PAGE 10 =====
       1.7  Dissertation  Structure  
Chapter  2  reviews  the  related  literature  within  four  domains  and  ties  each  one  to  the  one  of  the  
three
 
gaps.
 
Chapter
 
3
 
provides
 
a
 
detailed
 
list
 
of
 
the
 
formal
 
requirements.
 
Chapter
 
4
 
describes
 
design
 
and
 
rationales.
 
Chapter
 
5
 
deals
 
with
 
project
 
management.
 
Chapter
 
6
 
presents
 
experimental
 
results
 
and
 
reflections.
 
Chapter
 
7
 
provides
 
concluding
 
remarks.
 
 


===== PAGE 11 =====
       
2.  Literature  Review  and  Contextual  Analysis  
In  this  section,  the  literature  review  gaps  that  are  used  to  justify  each  design  choice  will  be  
outlined
 
in
 
relation
 
to
 
the
 
three
 
literature
 
gaps
 
stated
 
in
 
section
 
1.2.
 
Following
 
the
 
discussion
 
of
 
each
 
gap,
 
the
 
domain
 
context
 
will
 
also
 
be
 
outlined.
 
2.1  Concept-Based  Interpretability  -  Gap  1  
The  key  problem  posed  by  black-box  algorithms  in  clinical  practice  is  not  related  to  the  
quality
 
of
 
predictions,
 
which
 
is
 
generally
 
high
 
in
 
many
 
cases.
 
The
 
core
 
problem
 
lies
 
in
 
the
 
inability
 
to
 
question
 
the
 
reason
 
behind
 
any
 
particular
 
prediction.
 
TCAV
 
(Kim
 
et
 
al.,
 
2018)
 
provides
 
an
 
effective
 
solution
 
for
 
this
 
problem.
 
The
 
idea
 
of
 
this
 
approach
 
involves
 
training
 
a
 
linear
 
probe
 
that
 
establishes
 
connections
 
between
 
activation
 
vectors
 
in
 
a
 
layer
 
and
 
a
 
binary
 
indicator
 
of
 
concept
 
presence.
 
Subsequently,
 
this
 
vector
 
is
 
used
 
to
 
evaluate
 
how
 
sensitive
 
the
 
model
 
is
 
to
 
the
 
corresponding
 
concept.
 
The
 
critical
 
drawback
 
of
 
this
 
technique,
 
mentioned
 
by
 
the
 
authors,
 
is
 
the
 
need
 
for
 
hundreds
 
of
 
manually
 
labeled
 
examples
 
per
 
each
 
concept,
 
which
 
in
 
domains
 
such
 
as
 
medical
 
imaging,
 
where
 
the
 
list
 
of
 
possible
 
concepts
 
for
 
a
 
novel
 
disease
 
presentation
 
is
 
not
 
known
 
beforehand,
 
poses
 
an
 
insurmountable
 
constraint.
 
Concept  Bottleneck  Models  (Koh  et  al.,  2020)  solve  the  above  problem  by  enforcing  
predictions
 
to
 
pass
 
through
 
a
 
bottleneck
 
layer
 
with
 
encoded
 
concept
 
activations.
 
This
 
approach
 
allows
 
for
 
achieving
 
great
 
results
 
with
 
plenty
 
of
 
concept
 
supervision;
 
however,
 
without
 
it,
 
a
 
CBM
 
becomes
 
equivalent
 
to
 
a
 
black-box
 
model,
 
because
 
the
 
bottleneck
 
layer
 
learns
 
arbitrary
 
activations
 
and
 
ignores
 
any
 
semantics.
 
Unlike  TCAV,  ACE  (Ghorbani  et  al.,  2019)  does  not  require  a  pre-labeled  data  set  to  generate  
per-concept
 
explanations
 
because
 
it
 
uses
 
an
 
already
 
trained
 
classification
 
model
 
to
 
extract
 
concept
 
candidates
 
automatically.
 
The
 
approach
 
involves
 
cropping
 
the
 
input
 
image
 
into
 
superpixels
 
and
 
evaluating
 
each
 
of
 
them
 
according
 
to
 
the
 
TCAV
 
technique.
 
Still,
 
ACE
 
depends
 
on
 
segmentation:
 
it
 
needs
 
an
 
additional
 
algorithm
 
to
 
come
 
up
 
with
 
the
 
crop
 
suggestions.
 
Moreover,
 
this
 
problem
 
is
 
specifically
 
highlighted
 
by
 
the
 
authors
 
as
 
one
 
to
 
address
 
in
 
future
 
work.
 
In
 
the
 
present
 
case
 
study,
 
this
 
gap
 
is
 
addressed
 
by
 
leveraging
 
the
 
model’s
 
own
 
attention
 
weights
 
to
 
generate
 
the
 
patches.
 
While  STEGO  (Hamilton  et  al.,  2022)  proves  that  DINO’s  representation  is  rich  enough  to  
allow
 
for
 
unsupervised
 
segmentation
 
into
 
semantically
 
coherent
 
regions,
 
it
 
stops
 
here
 
without
 


===== PAGE 12 =====
       providing  a  naming  pipeline,  a  concept-based  classifier,  and  per-instance  explanations.  The  
latter
 
problem,
 
together
 
with
 
naming,
 
makes
 
up
 
the
 
core
 
of
 
the
 
current
 
research.
 
2.2  Self-Supervised  Visual  Encoders  -  Gap  2  (Image  Domain)  
Contrastive  self-supervised  techniques,  exemplified  by  SimCLR  (Chen  et  al.,  2020)  and  
MoCo
 
(He
 
et
 
al.,
 
2020),
 
provided
 
a
 
breakthrough
 
in
 
learning
 
visual
 
representation
 
without
 
ground
 
truth
 
labels.
 
Such
 
algorithms
 
were
 
intended
 
for
 
global
 
image-level
 
representations
 
without
 
any
 
specific
 
objectives
 
for
 
spatial
 
part
 
representations.
 
The  next  milestone  was  reached  by  DINO  (Caron  et  al.,  2021),  who  have  reported  surprising  
results:
 
self-distillation
 
objective
 
yielded
 
attention
 
maps
 
for
 
[CLS]
 
tokens
 
segmenting
 
foreground
 
objects,
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
patch
 
features
 
clustering
 
according
 
to
 
semantics
 
without
 
any
 
part-based
 
supervision.
 
The
 
authors
 
report
 
this
 
fact
 
and
 
state
 
that
 
the
 
structure
 
of
 
layers
 
is
 
"left
 
unexplored."
 
In
 
some
 
sense,
 
the
 
current
 
research
 
project
 
may
 
be
 
viewed
 
as
 
an
 
embodiment
 
of
 
this
 
gesture
 
made
 
but
 
left
 
unexplored
 
by
 
DINO’s
 
authors.
 
Instead  of  feature  extraction  in  the  last  block,  as  Amir  et  al.  (2022)  do  in  their  ViT-based  part  
discovery
 
task,
 
the
 
features
 
will
 
be
 
extracted
 
in
 
Blocks
 
8,
 
10,
 
and
 
12
 
and
 
concatenated
 
into
 
a
 
single
 
vector
 
of
 
size
 
1,152.
 
It
 
will
 
be
 
assumed
 
that
 
Block
 
8
 
contains
 
features
 
encoding
 
textures,
 
Block
 
10
 
encodes
 
part
 
outline
 
features,
 
while
 
Block
 
12
 
encodes
 
semantics
 
of
 
part
 
identity.
 
The
 
corresponding
 
ablation
 
variant
 
(4.5)
 
will
 
test
 
whether
 
this
 
assumption
 
holds
 
true.
 
The  foreground  masking  procedure  in  DIFE  will  follow  the  LEOPART  pipeline  (Ziegler  and  
Asano,
 
2022),
 
using
 
[CLS]
 
attention
 
density
 
for
 
DINO
 
to
 
separate
 
foreground
 
objects
 
from
 
the
 
background
 
before
 
performing
 
clustering.
 
Specifically,
 
the
 
threshold
 
θ
 
=
 
0.75
 
will
 
be
 
used,
 
selected
 
via
 
a
 
grid
 
search
 
against
 
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pet
 
Dataset
 
annotations
 
(mean
 
IoU
 
0.73
 
±
 
0.09).
 
At
 
the
 
same
 
time,
 
Siméoni
 
et
 
al.'s
 
LOST
 
(2021)
 
independently
 
confirms
 
that
 
ViT-based
 
patch
 
features
 
support
 
localization
 
without
 
supervision.
 
An  interesting  twist  to  this  technique  is  that  [CLS]  attention  is  used  here  only  as  a  binary  
indicator,
 
selecting
 
foreground
 
patches,
 
not
 
as
 
an
 
explanation
 
of
 
the
 
importance
 
of
 
features
 
inside
 
it.
 
It
 
resolves
 
the
 
controversy
 
over
 
interpretation
 
of
 
attention
 
weights
 
(see
 
Jain
 
and
 
Wallace,
 
2019;
 
Wiegreffe
 
and
 
Pinter,
 
2019).
 
 


===== PAGE 13 =====
       2.3  Tabular  Deep  Learning  and  Panel  Structure  -  Gap  2  (Tabular  Domain)  
The  FT-Transformer  model  (Gorishniy  et  al.,  2021)  continues  to  dominate  tabular  deep  
learning.
 
The
 
key
 
idea
 
behind
 
it—the
 
use
 
of
 
individual
 
linear
 
projections
 
specific
 
to
 
a
 
task,
 
rather
 
than
 
shared
 
embeddings,
 
to
 
represent
 
each
 
of
 
the
 
features—finds
 
strong
 
justification,
 
since
 
different
 
physiological
 
states
 
correspond
 
to
 
different
 
deviations,
 
e.g.,
 
2σ
 
shift
 
in
 
glucose
 
vs.
 
2σ
 
shift
 
in
 
creatinine,
 
and
 
therefore,
 
shared
 
embeddings
 
make
 
little
 
sense
 
here.
 
At
 
the
 
same
 
time,
 
the
 
FT-Transformer
 
does
 
not
 
take
 
into
 
account
 
the
 
structure
 
of
 
the
 
dataset
 
or
 
clinical
 
interdependencies
 
between
 
features,
 
which
 
is
 
duly
 
recognized
 
by
 
its
 
developers.
 
Lab-MAE  (Restrepo  et  al.,  2025)  utilizes  masked  autoencoding  for  tabular  data,  following  the  
correct
 
overall
 
strategy
 
for
 
self-supervised
 
pre-training
 
on
 
laboratory
 
panels.
 
Lab-MAE
 
suffers,
 
however,
 
from
 
one
 
drawback
 
associated
 
with
 
a
 
purely
 
random
 
masking
 
procedure:
 
Lab-MAE
 
masks
 
individual
 
values
 
regardless
 
of
 
their
 
panel
 
affiliation,
 
which
 
overlooks
 
the
 
inherent
 
structure
 
of
 
blood
 
tests
 
(CBC,
 
BMP,
 
Lipid),
 
corresponding
 
to
 
the
 
physiology
 
of
 
distinct
 
physiological
 
systems.
 
The  proposed  PanelFTTransformer  model  (PSFTT)  is  based  on  the  FT-Transformer  
framework
 
with
 
panel
 
membership
 
encoded
 
explicitly
 
and
 
a
 
self-supervised
 
training
 
scheme
 
exploiting
 
this
 
panel
 
structure.
 
PSFTT
 
uses
 
two
 
levels
 
of
 
self-supervised
 
pre-training,
 
and
 
intermediate
 
representation
 
layers
 
(L2,
 
L4,
 
and
 
L6)
 
are
 
used
 
for
 
concept
 
clustering—apparently
 
for
 
the
 
first
 
time
 
in
 
case
 
of
 
FT-Transformers.
 
The  basic  problem  faced  by  both  FT-Transformer,  Lab-MAE,  and  related  architectures  is  that  
they
 
consider
 
the
 
tabular
 
input
 
as
 
a
 
single
 
sequence
 
of
 
feature
 
values,
 
without
 
taking
 
into
 
account
 
the
 
fact
 
that
 
laboratory
 
tests
 
have
 
a
 
natural
 
organization
 
in
 
panels.
 
The
 
result
 
of
 
a
 
CBC
 
test
 
can
 
be
 
considered
 
a
 
panel
 
since
 
the
 
values
 
contained
 
in
 
it
 
correlate
 
in
 
a
 
specific
 
way,
 
and
 
an
 
example
 
of
 
that
 
is
 
anaemia,
 
which
 
appears
 
through
 
correlated
 
values
 
of
 
haemoglobin,
 
MCV,
 
and
 
RDW.
 
Using
 
typical
 
feature
 
tokenization
 
and
 
random
 
masking
 
leads
 
to
 
a
 
representation
 
that
 
is
 
statistically
 
valid
 
but
 
does
 
not
 
contain
 
any
 
clinically
 
meaningful
 
interpretation.
 
This
 
problem
 
precisely
 
represents
 
what
 
PSFTT
 
addresses
 
through
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
a
 
panel
 
positional
 
embedding
 
and
 
two
 
scales
 
for
 
SSL.
 
2.4  LLM-Mediated  Concept  Validation  —  Gap  3  
A  concise  but  valuable  work  is  ClusterLLM  (Zhang  et  al.,  2023).  The  idea  behind  
ClusterLLM
 
can
 
be
 
easily
 
summarized:
 
LLMs
 
have
 
enough
 
world
 
knowledge
 
to
 
determine
 
if
 


===== PAGE 14 =====
       a  group  of  text  items  is  coherent  semantically.  The  model  showed  good  performance  on  
textual
 
data.
 
It
 
should
 
be
 
noted
 
that
 
Zhang
 
et
 
al.
 
explicitly
 
indicate
 
numerical
 
and
 
clinical
 
domains
 
as
 
those
 
not
 
covered
 
at
 
the
 
moment:
 
“extending
 
to
 
non-textual
 
domains
 
where
 
LLM
 
input
 
formatting
 
is
 
non-trivial
 
remains
 
an
 
open
 
challenge.”
 
The  use  of  the  LLM  in  the  context  of  this  particular  project  tackles  precisely  this  issue.  
Instead
 
of
 
providing
 
raw
 
numeric
 
values
 
to
 
the
 
LLM,
 
which
 
would
 
confuse
 
it,
 
a
 
deviation
 
profile
 
for
 
every
 
cluster
 
is
 
calculated.
 
In
 
other
 
words,
 
the
 
results
 
of
 
each
 
laboratory
 
test
 
are
 
classified
 
as
 
very
 
elevated,
 
slightly
 
elevated,
 
normal,
 
slightly
 
lowered,
 
or
 
very
 
lowered
 
in
 
relation
 
to
 
the
 
clinical
 
norm.
 
This
 
deviation
 
profile
 
is
 
rephrased
 
into
 
a
 
structured
 
clinical
 
question
 
for
 
the
 
LLM,
 
which
 
generates
 
a
 
proposed
 
label,
 
differential
 
diagnoses,
 
and
 
a
 
confidence
 
score.
 
After
 
that,
 
the
 
suggestions
 
are
 
evaluated,
 
amended,
 
or
 
rejected
 
by
 
the
 
human
 
domain
 
expert.
 
Providing  numerical  data  as  a  deviation  profile  seems  to  be  the  key  point  of  the  whole  
approach.
 
Initially,
 
attempts
 
to
 
provide
 
the
 
numbers
 
in
 
the
 
raw
 
format
 
led
 
to
 
non-specific
 
and
 
evasive
 
answers
 
from
 
the
 
LLM.
 
However,
 
a
 
carefully
 
crafted
 
structure,
 
ordered
 
according
 
to
 
the
 
degree
 
of
 
deviation
 
and
 
explicitly
 
including
 
the
 
normal
 
range,
 
yielded
 
much
 
more
 
relevant
 
and
 
concrete
 
recommendations.
 
Even
 
though
 
the
 
creation
 
of
 
prompts
 
involved
 
more
 
work
 
than
 
expected,
 
it
 
paid
 
off.
 
2.5  Domain  Knowledge  Integration  Taxonomy  
According  to  Von  Rueden  et  al.  (2021),  domain  knowledge  integration  is  said  to  operate  on  
three
 
levels:
 
input
 
(feature
 
engineering),
 
architecture
 
(inductive
 
biases),
 
and
 
loss
 
(auxiliary
 
objectives).
 
Similarly,
 
Dash
 
et
 
al.
 
(2022)
 
arrive
 
at
 
the
 
same
 
taxonomy,
 
further
 
proving
 
with
 
evidence
 
from
 
various
 
domains
 
that
 
the
 
combined
 
effect
 
of
 
all
 
three
 
levels
 
of
 
domain
 
knowledge
 
consistently
 
surpasses
 
the
 
effects
 
achieved
 
by
 
any
 
of
 
them
 
individually.
 
This
 
framework
 
is
 
also
 
included
 
in
 
our
 
DIFE
 
pipeline
 
through
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
all
 
three
 
components.
 
In
 
terms
 
of
 
inputs,
 
clinical
 
deviation
 
encoding
 
of
 
tabular
 
data
 
is
 
used
 
to
 
make
 
sure
 
that
 
scales
 
are
 
calibrated
 
not
 
only
 
in
 
terms
 
of
 
statistics
 
but
 
clinical
 
importance
 
as
 
well.
 
In
 
terms
 
of
 
architecture,
 
foreground
 
attention
 
mask
 
for
 
image
 
inputs
 
and
 
panel
 
position
 
embedding
 
for
 
tabular
 
data
 
are
 
used.
 
Losses
 
include
 
semantic
 
consistency,
 
fine-tuning
 
of
 
image
 
inputs
 
and
 
two-scale
 
self-supervised
 
learning
 
of
 
tabular
 
data.
 
 


===== PAGE 15 =====
       2.6  Critical  Gap  Analysis  
Table  1  below  positions  DIFE  against  related  work  across  the  dimensions  that  matter  for  this  
project.
 
Method
 
Supervision
 
Part
 
Discovery
 Named  
Concepts
 
Per-Image  
Explanation
 
Gaps
 
Addressed
 
TCAV  (Kim  et  al.,  
2018)
 
Supervised  +  
concept
 
labels
 
Partial
 Yes  
(manual)
 
No
 
None
 
—
 
introduces
 
Gap
 
1
 
CBM
 
(Koh
 
et
 
al.,
 
2020)
 Supervised  +  
concept
 
labels
 
Yes
 Yes  
(manual)
 
Yes
 
None
 
—
 
introduces
 
Gap
 
1
 
ACE  (Ghorbani  et  al.,  
2019)
 
Supervised  +  
segmentation
 
Yes
 
Partial
 
No
 
None
 
—
 
introduces
 
Gap
 
1
 
STEGO  (Hamilton  et  
al.,
 
2022)
 
Self-supervised
 
Yes
 
No
 
No
 
None
 
—
 
no
 
naming
 
pipeline
 
Amir
 
et
 
al.
 
(2022)
 
Self-supervised
 
Yes
 
(single
 
layer)
 
No
 
No
 
Partial
 
Gap
 
2
 
ClusterLLM  (Zhang  et  
al.,
 
2023)
 
Self-supervised
 
Text
 
only
 
Yes
 
(text)
 
No
 
None
 
—
 
introduces
 
Gap
 
3
 
FT-Transformer  
(Gorishniy,
 
2021)
 
Supervised  
(tabular)
 
No
 
No
 
No
 None  —  introduces  Gap  2  
(tabular)
 
DIFE
 
—
 
This
 
work
 Self-supervised  
only
 
Yes
 
(multi-layer)
 Yes  
(post-hoc)
 
Yes
 
Gaps
 
1,
 
2,
 
3
 
—
 
all
 
three
 
Table
 
1:
 
Gap
 
analysis
 
—
 
position
 
of
 
DIFE
 
against
 
related
 
work
 
DIFE  is  the  only  approach  among  the  comparative  studies  discussed  that  requires  no  labels  to  
be
 
trained,
 
generates
 
named
 
concepts,
 
offers
 
instance-specific
 
explanations,
 
and
 
can
 
handle
 
images
 
and
 
tables.
 
It
 
is
 
this
 
combination
 
that
 
forms
 
the
 
core
 
of
 
the
 
argument
 
presented
 
in
 
this
 
dissertation.
 
Three  key  insights  come  out  of  Table  1  and  need  explicit  clarification.  The  first  insight  is  that  
no
 
current
 
technique
 
simultaneously
 
solves
 
the
 
three
 
issues:
 
each
 
solution
 
currently
 
exists
 
that
 
solves
 
one
 
or
 
two
 
gaps
 
but
 
leaves
 
others
 
unsolved.
 
TCAV
 
and
 
CBM
 
solve
 
concept-level
 
interpretability,
 
but
 
they
 
rely
 
on
 
labeling.
 
ACE
 
solves
 
the
 
labeling
 
problem
 
but
 
generates
 
anonymous
 
clusters.
 
Lastly,
 
DINO
 
and
 
FT-Transformer
 
generate
 
rich
 
representations,
 
but
 
concept
 
structures
 
were
 
never
 
studied
 
within
 
the
 
intermediate
 
layers
 
of
 
these
 
transformers.
 
DIFE
 
becomes
 
the
 
first
 
solution
 
pipeline
 
to
 
combine
 
these
 
strengths
 
without
 
relying
 
on
 
concept
 
supervision.
 
The  second  insight  stems  from  the  fact  that  the  table’s  column  dedicated  to  the  tabular  data  
field
 
is
 
by
 
far
 
the
 
emptiest.
 
In
 
truth,
 
literature
 
bias
 
exists
 
here
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
research
 
in
 
the
 
area
 
of
 
interpretable
 
machine
 
learning
 
is
 
largely
 
focused
 
on
 
image
 
and
 
textual
 
data,
 
failing
 
to
 
consider
 
clinical
 
numerical
 
data
 
adequately.
 


===== PAGE 16 =====
       Finally,  instance-level  explanation  -  identifying  which  concepts  influenced  the  decision  on  a  
patient’s
 
record
 
-
 
is
 
a
 
capability
 
not
 
offered
 
by
 
any
 
of
 
the
 
techniques
 
discussed
 
except
 
for
 
DIFE.
 
 


===== PAGE 17 =====
       
3.  Problem  Description  and  Requirements  
3.1  Problem  Statement  
Problem  statement:  Given  an  input  domain  where  interpretability  of  predictions  is  required,  
propose
 
a
 
system
 
that
 
(1)
 
detects
 
recurrent
 
semantic
 
patterns
 
without
 
the
 
help
 
of
 
labeled
 
concepts,
 
(2)
 
facilitates
 
a
 
process
 
by
 
which
 
the
 
domain
 
expert
 
can
 
name
 
these
 
patterns
 
after
 
training
 
in
 
a
 
lightweight
 
post-hoc
 
step,
 
and
 
(3)
 
uses
 
these
 
named
 
patterns
 
for
 
prediction
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
can
 
be
 
traced,
 
audited,
 
and
 
contested
 
by
 
the
 
expert.
 
The  following  failure  modes  define  the  space  of  designs.  The  first  failure  mode  is  
labeling-at-training-time,
 
where
 
TCAV
 
and
 
CBMs
 
need
 
concept
 
labels
 
before
 
or
 
during
 
training;
 
however,
 
if
 
we
 
do
 
not
 
know
 
the
 
concepts
 
or
 
it
 
is
 
costly
 
to
 
get
 
annotations
 
before
 
training,
 
it
 
will
 
not
 
work.
 
The
 
second
 
failure
 
mode
 
is
 
the
 
naming
 
problem,
 
where
 
methods
 
using
 
DINO
 
detect
 
semantically
 
consistent
 
clusters
 
but
 
only
 
label
 
them
 
with
 
an
 
integer
 
like
 
“cluster
 
7,”
 
which
 
is
 
meaningless
 
to
 
a
 
medical
 
practitioner.
 
The
 
design
 
proposal
 
above
 
avoids
 
both
 
failure
 
modes.
 
The  problem  described  in  this  paper  is  related  to  CS4700  Project  Specification  
"
04_Interpretable_Space.pdf
,"
 
as
 
provided
 
by
 
Dr.
 
Roberto
 
Alamino.
 
3.2  Functional  Requirements  
 
FR#
 
Requirement
 
Description
 
and
 
Acceptance
 
Criterion
 
FR1
 Multi-layer  DINO  
Feature
 
Extraction
 
Extract  patch  features  from  ViT-S/8  blocks  8,  10,  12;  concatenate  to  1152-dim.  AC:  output  shape  
(N_images
 
×
 
784
 
×
 
1152);
 
assertion
 
verified
 
on
 
first
 
run.
 
FR2
 Attention-Guided  
Foreground
 
Masking
 
Threshold  CLS-to-patch  attention  at  θ=0.75  to  retain  top  25%  of  patches.  AC:  mean  IoU  vs  
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pet
 
segmentation
 
mask
 
≥
 
0.70
 
on
 
100-image
 
sample.
 
FR3
 PCA  +  Spatial  
Augmentation
 
Reduce  1152→128  dims  via  PCA;  append  weighted  (row/28,  col/28)  coordinates.  AC:  
cumulative
 
explained
 
variance
 
≥
 
85%;
 
augmented
 
dim
 
=
 
130.
 
FR4
 
K-Selection
 
Diagnostics
 Compute  WCSS  and  silhouette  for  K  ∈  [K_min,  K_max];  recommend  argmax(silhouette).  AC:  diagnostic  plots  saved;  recommended  K  
returned
 
as
 
scalar.
 
FR5
 GMM  Concept  
Clustering
 
Fit  diagonal-covariance  GMM  with  K  components;  produce  hard  and  soft  assignments.  AC:  
convergence
 
within
 
200
 
EM
 
iterations;
 
assignments
 
cover
 
all
 
foreground
 
patches.
 
FR6
 Semantic  Consistency  
Fine-Tuning
 
Fine-tune  last  2  DINO  blocks  using  GMM  pseudo-labels  as  contrastive  pairs;  no  ground-truth  labels.  AC:  post-FT  silhouette  ≥  pre-FT  
silhouette
 
on
 
validation
 
set.
 
FR7
 Human  Concept  
Labelling
 
UI
 
(Image)
 
Streamlit  interface  showing  ≥16  representative  patches  per  cluster;  accepts  label,  confidence,  include/exclude.  AC:  labels.json  written  
correctly.
 
FR8
 PanelFTTransformer  
Architecture
 
FeatureTokenizer  (T_i  =  x_i  *  W_i  +  b_i)  +  panel  positional  encoding  (learned  nn.Embedding  for  3  
panels)
 
+
 
6
 
TransformerEncoder
 
layers.
 
AC:
 
panel-patch
 
output
 
shape
 
(N_records
 
×
 
3
 
×
 
384).
 


===== PAGE 18 =====
       
FR#
 
Requirement
 
Description
 
and
 
Acceptance
 
Criterion
 
FR9
 Two-Scale  SSL  
Pre-Training
 
(Lab)
 
Within-panel  masking  (40%  of  tests  in  one  panel)  +  cross-panel  dropout  (drop  entire  panel;  CLS  
predicts
 
panel
 
mean).
 
AC:
 
pre-training
 
loss
 
converges;
 
NMI
 
improves
 
vs.
 
no
 
pre-training.
 
FR10
 LLM  Clinical  Concept  
Advisor
 
Build  deviation  profile  per  cluster;  query  LLM  for  clinical  label  +  rationale;  present  to  human  for  validation.  AC:  acceptance  rate  
logged;
 
≥70%
 
accepted
 
without
 
modification.
 
FR11
 Concept  Activation  
Scoring
 
Compute  cluster-proportion  score  per  image/record.  AC:  score  matrix  
shape
 
(N
 
×
 
K);
 
scores
 
sum
 
to
 
≤1.0.
 
FR12
 Concept-Based  
Classification
 
Train  OneClassSVM  (image)  or  LogReg  (tabular)  on  concept  score  vectors;  evaluate  AUC-ROC,  
F1.
 
AC:
 
results
 
written
 
to
 
metrics.json;
 
compared
 
to
 
raw-feature
 
baseline.
 
FR13
 Per-Instance  
Explanation
 
Generate  3-panel  figure:  original  image  |  semantic  concept  map  |  
concept
 
contribution
 
bar
 
chart.
 
AC:
 
generated
 
in
 
≤5
 
seconds
 
on
 
CPU.
 
Table
 
2:
 
Functional
 
Requirements
 
Summary
 
3.3  Non-Functional  Requirements  
 
NFR#
 
Category
 
Requirement
 
NFR1
 
Performance
 Image  feature  extraction:  ≤30  min  for  1,200  images  on  Apple  MPS  or  CUDA;  ≤90  min  on  CPU.  Lab  pre-training:  runnable  on  CPU  in  ≤4  
hours
 
at
 
reduced
 
epoch
 
count.
 
NFR2
 
Reproducibility
 All  results  reproducible  from  the  same  random_seed  in  config.yaml.  No  randomised  
operations
 
outside
 
seeded
 
contexts.
 
NFR3
 
Portability
 Runs  on  macOS  (MPS),  Linux  (CUDA),  Windows  (CPU)  with  no  code  changes  beyond  the  
device
 
field
 
in
 
config.yaml.
 
NFR4
 
Modularity
 Each  pipeline  stage  independently  re-executable  without  re-running  prior  stages,  via  
file-system
 
cache.
 
NFR5
 Domain  
Generality
 
The  same  downstream  pipeline  (clustering,  labelling,  scoring,  classification)  must  execute  
identically
 
on
 
both
 
image
 
and
 
tabular
 
encoders
 
with
 
no
 
stage-specific
 
code.
 
NFR6
 
Explainability
 All  model  decisions  traceable  through  the  concept  activation  vector  framework.  No  
black-box
 
components
 
after
 
feature
 
extraction.
 
NFR7
 
Privacy
 
No
 
personal
 
data
 
processed.
 
No
 
external
 
API
 
calls
 
for
 
data.
 
All
 
computation
 
local.
 
Table
 
3:
 
Non-Functional
 
Requirements
 
 


===== PAGE 19 =====
       
4.  Practical  Realisation:  Design,  Implementation  and  Testing  
This  chapter  describes  the  development  and  reasoning  behind  the  work.  Each  design  choice  is  
associated
 
with
 
one
 
of
 
the
 
three
 
gaps
 
discovered
 
in
 
the
 
literature.
 
Section
 
4.2
 
and
 
Section
 
4.3
 
cover
 
the
 
image
 
and
 
tabular
 
pipelines,
 
respectively;
 
Section
 
4.4
 
covers
 
the
 
shared
 
downstream
 
modules;
 
and
 
Section
 
4.5
 
covers
 
the
 
experimental
 
design.
 
 
Figure  3:  DIFE  pipeline  —  nine  stages  applied  identically  across  image  and  tabular  domains.   
4.1  Shared  Architecture  Overview  
Both  pipelines  share  a  common  approach  with  three  main  components:  domain-aware  
embedding,
 
clustering,
 
and
 
semantic
 
annotation.
 
The
 
downstream
 
pipeline
 
of
 
clustering,
 
labeling,
 
ranking
 
of
 
concepts,
 
and
 
classification
 
uses
 
the
 
same
 
software
 
in
 
both
 
cases.
 
The
 
purposeful
 
choice
 
is
 
made
 
to
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
test
 
NFR5
 
(domain
 
generality)
 
empirically
 
rather
 
than
 
just
 
claim
 
that
 
the
 
requirement
 
is
 
satisfied.
 
Stage
 
Image
 
Domain
 
(DINO)
 
Tabular
 
Domain
 
(PSFTT)
 
Pillar
 
Encode
 DINO  ViT-S/8,  blocks  8/10/12  →  1152-dim  
patch
 
features
 
PSFTT:  FeatureTokenizer  +  panel  PE,  layers  
L2/L4/L6
 
→
 
384-dim
 
panel-patches
 
2
 
Filter
 CLS  attention  masking:  retain  top  25%  patches  
(θ=0.75)
 
Clinical  deviation  encoding:  normalise  to  signed  
deviation
 
from
 
normal
 
range
 
2
 


===== PAGE 20 =====
       
Stage
 
Image
 
Domain
 
(DINO)
 
Tabular
 
Domain
 
(PSFTT)
 
Pillar
 
Cluster
 GMM  (30  components,  diag  covariance)  on  
PCA(128)
 
+
 
spatial(2)
 
=
 
130-dim
 
Per-panel  GMM  (10  components  ×  3  panels  =  30)  
on
 
PCA(20)
 
features
 
1
 
Label
 Human  expert  names  clusters  via  Streamlit  UI  
(label_tool.py)
 
LLM  Clinical  Advisor  suggests  name;  human  
validates
 
(lab_label_tool.py)
 
3
 
Score
 Cluster-proportion  score:  fraction  of  patches  per  
concept
 
per
 
image
 
Soft  GMM  membership  probability  per  concept  per  
patient
 
record
 
1
 
Classify
 LogReg  on  concept  scores;  explain  via  3-panel  
figure
 
LogReg  on  concept  scores;  compare  to  raw-feature  
LogReg
 
baseline
 
1+2+3
 
Table
 
4:
 
Two-Domain
 
Architecture
 
—
 
same
 
three
 
pillars
 
applied
 
across
 
both
 
modalities
 
4.2  Image  Domain  —  DINO  Pipeline  
4.2.1  Dataset  and  Preprocessing  
The  STL-10  dataset  (Coates  et  al.,  2011)  consists  of  three  classes,  namely  cat,  car,  and  bird,  
with
 
about
 
1,300
 
samples
 
each.
 
Cats
 
form
 
the
 
positive
 
class
 
in
 
this
 
one-class
 
classification
 
model,
 
similar
 
to
 
the
 
disease-positive
 
state
 
in
 
medicine.
 
The
 
quality
 
of
 
foreground
 
masking
 
was
 
evaluated
 
using
 
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pet
 
segmentation
 
masks
 
(Parkhi
 
et
 
al.,
 
2012)
 
on
 
100
 
cat
 
images
 
that
 
were
 
not
 
used
 
in
 
the
 
training
 
phase.
 
The
 
preprocessing
 
process
 
consisted
 
of
 
the
 
following
 
steps:
 
resizing
 
to
 
256,
 
center
 
cropping
 
to
 
224,
 
transformation
 
to
 
tensor
 
format,
 
and
 
normalization
 
using
 
the
 
ImageNet
 
mean
 
and
 
standard
 
deviation.
 
4.2.2  Multi-Layer  Feature  Extraction  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Architecture)  
The  DINO  ViT-S/8  model  uses  a  224×224  image  which  is  divided  into  784  patch  tokens  and  
passed
 
through
 
12
 
layers.
 
The
 
model
 
does
 
not
 
just
 
use
 
the
 
output
 
layer
 
as
 
the
 
source
 
of
 
features
 
but
 
extracts
 
features
 
from
 
three
 
intermediate
 
layers,
 
features
 
from
 
three
 
intermediate
 
layers
 
are
 
concatenated
 
as
 
follows:
 
                                                                                                                                   
  feats  =  torch.cat([all_layers[i][:,  1:,  :]  for  i  in  (0,  2,  4)],   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
dim=-1)
            #  indices  0,2,4  of  last-5  =  blocks  7,9,11  =  layers  8,10,12    Each  patch  feature  vector  will  be  1152  dimensional.  Block  8  encodes  the  mid-level  texture  
structures,
 
Block
 
10
 
encodes
 
part
 
outline
 
information,
 
whereas
 
Block
 
12
 
encodes
 
the
 
part
 
identity
 
like
 
“this
 
is
 
the
 
cat’s
 
ear.”
 
In
 
variant
 
B
 
I
 
intend
 
to
 
check
 
if
 
using
 
the
 
concatenation
 
of
 
features
 
results
 
in
 
better
 
clustering
 
of
 
concepts
 
than
 
using
 
only
 
Block
 
12.
 
4.2.3  Attention-Guided  Foreground  Masking  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  1,  Architecture)  
The  ACE  paper  by  Ghorbani  et  al.  (2019)  highlights  the  elimination  of  dependency  on  
pre-defined
 
segmentation
 
as
 
potential
 
research
 
directions.
 
In
 
this
 
study,
 
foreground
 
masks
 
are
 


===== PAGE 21 =====
       generated  using  the  attention  scores  of  the  inherent  [CLS]  token  from  the  DINO  model  across  
all
 
six
 
heads
 
of
 
the
 
final
 
transformer
 
layer:
 
attn  =  model.get_last_selfattention(image)    #  (1,  6,  785,  785)  cls_attn  =  attn[0,  :,  0,  1:].mean(0)         #  mean  over  heads  ->  
(784,)
 mask  =  cls_attn  >  threshold(cls_attn,  0.75)   #  retain  top  25%   θ=0.75  was  selected  by  grid  search  over  {0.5,  0.6,  0.7,  0.75,  0.8}  against  Oxford-IIIT  Pet  
segmentation
 
mask
 
annotations,
 
achieving
 
mean
 
IoU
 
0.73
 
(±0.09)
 
—
 
the
 
highest
 
among
 
all
 
tested
 
values.
 
4.2.4  GMM  Concept  Clustering  (Pillar  1  —  Gap  1)  
After  applying  PCA  to  reduce  the  number  of  dimensions  from  1152  to  128,  while  retaining  
85.3
 
percent
 
of
 
variance
 
and
 
after
 
adding
 
the
 
spatial
 
dimension,
 
which
 
was
 
surprisingly
 
more
 
effective
 
than
 
expected
 
for
 
preventing
 
texture-based
 
fusion,
 
we
 
fit
 
the
 
data
 
into
 
the
 
Gaussian
 
Mixture
 
Model
 
with
 
Diagonal
 
Covariance
 
Matrix.
 
    gmm  =  GaussianMixture(n_components=30,  covariance_type=’diag’         ,n_init  =3)  gmm.fit(X_aug[subsample_150k])   #  subsample  for  tractability   The  number  K=30  is  derived  from  the  K-selection  process  mentioned  in  Section  4.5.1.  GMM  
is
 
used
 
instead
 
of
 
k-means
 
clustering
 
for
 
some
 
advantages
 
such
 
as
 
handling
 
unequal
 
cluster
 
sizes
 
(e.g.,
 
those
 
undergone
 
by
 
eye
 
patches
 
compared
 
to
 
fur
 
patches);
 
it
 
provides
 
probability-based
 
cluster
 
membership
 
which
 
is
 
ideal
 
for
 
concept
 
scoring;
 
and
 
it
 
does
 
not
 
assume
 
spherical
 
clusters.
 
The
 
spatial
 
smoothing
 
method
 
involves
 
using
 
the
 
majority
 
vote
 
filter
 
on
 
each
 
patch’s
 
3×3
 
neighborhood
 
and
 
is
 
performed
 
using
 
the
 
smooth_labels()
 
function
 
found
 
in
 
patch_clusterer.py
.
 
4.2.5  Semantic  Consistency  Fine-Tuning  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Loss)  
The  fine-tuning  stage  employs  the  obtained  cluster  labels  to  promote  the  convergence  of  the  
patch
 
representations
 
within
 
a
 
cluster
 
while
 
diverging
 
those
 
belonging
 
to
 
distinct
 
clusters:
 
L_sem  =  sum_{same  pairs}  ||f(xi)  -  f(xj)||^2  -  lambda  *  sum_{diff  
pairs}
 
||f(xi)
 
-
 
f(xj)||^2
  During  the  training  process,  only  the  last  two  transformer  layers  remain  unfrozen.  AdamW  is  
employed
 
for
 
optimization
 
at
 
a
 
learning
 
rate
 
of
 
1e-5,
 
a
 
weight
 
decay
 
factor
 
of
 
0.01,
 
and
 
a
 
gradient
 
clip
 
of
 
1.0
 
over
 
three
 
epochs.
 
This
 
creates
 
an
 
iterative
 
process:
 
initial
 
DINO
 
→
 


===== PAGE 22 =====
       clustering  →  fine-tuning  →  better  DINO  →  better  clusters.  The  ablation  study  (Variant  D)  
demonstrates
 
a
 
positive
 
impact
 
on
 
geometric
 
tightness
 
but
 
introduces
 
a
 
slight
 
sacrifice
 
in
 
terms
 
of
 
class
 
discriminability,
 
which
 
is
 
elaborated
 
upon
 
in
 
Section
 
6.1
 
4.2.6  Concept  Labelling  and  Activation  Scoring  (Pillar  3  —  Gap  1)  
After  applying  clustering,  the  subject  matter  expert  assigns  nomenclature  to  each  cluster  based  
on
 
representative
 
patches
 
and
 
their
 
respective
 
concept
 
map
 
through
 
the
 
Streamlit
 
labeling
 
tool.
 
Nomenclatures
 
such
 
as
 
“cat:ear”,
 
“cat:eye”
 
and
 
“cat:fur”
 
are
 
cached
 
in
 
cache/labels.json
 
file
 
and
 
are
 
applied
 
to
 
subsequent
 
steps.
 
 
Cluster  activations  represent  proportions  in  which  clusters  account  for  foreground  patches  in  
each
 
image—how
 
much
 
of
 
the
 
image
 
is
 
attributed
 
to
 
each
 
named
 
concept
 
cluster.
 
While
 
cosine
 
similarities
 
were
 
considered
 
based
 
on
 
cosine
 
similarities
 
to
 
the
 
concept
 
vector,
 
these
 
scores
 
ranged
 
from
 
0.4
 
to
 
0.8
 
without
 
any
 
discriminative
 
powers.
 
However,
 
proportions
 
yield
 
real
 
zeros
 
when
 
concepts
 
are
 
missing,
 
a
 
feature
 
essential
 
to
 
downstream
 
classifier
 
performance.
 
4.3  Tabular  Domain  —  PanelFTTransformer  (PSFTT)  Pipeline  
4.3.1  Dataset  
NHANES  2013-2018  includes  three  cycles  of  combined  surveys  which  result  in  18,673  data  
instances.
 
Each
 
instance
 
involves
 
34
 
laboratory
 
measurements
 
organized
 
in
 
three
 
categories,
 
viz.,
 
Complete
 
Blood
 
Count
 
(CBC)
 
with
 
14
 
features
 
(white
 
blood
 
cells,
 
hemoglobin,
 
mean
 
corpuscular
 
volume,
 
and
 
platelets);
 
Basic
 
Metabolic
 
Panel
 
(BMP)
 
with
 
12
 
features
 
(glucose,
 
creatinine,
 
sodium,
 
and
 
potassium);
 
and
 
the
 
Lipid
 
panel
 
with
 
8
 
features
 
(LDL,
 
HDL,
 
total
 
cholesterol,
 
and
 
triglycerides).
 
Diagnosis
 
codes
 
generated
 
by
 
NHANES
 
questionnaires
 
are
 
considered
 
exclusively
 
in
 
the
 
process
 
of
 
classification
 
and
 
not
 
during
 
pre-training,
 
coding,
 
and
 
clustering.
 
4.3.2  Clinical  Deviation  Encoding  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Input)  
Rather  than  using  a  z-score,  each  lab  measure  is  coded  as  the  positive  or  negative  difference  
from
 
its
 
clinical
 
normal
 
range:
 
dev_i  =  0                          if  x_i  in  [low_i,  high_i]  dev_i  =  (x_i  -  high_i)  /  sigma_i   if  x_i  >  high_i  dev_i  =  (low_i  -  x_i)  /  sigma_i    if  x_i  <  low_i   


===== PAGE 23 =====
       The  clinical  dichotomy  of  “normal”  vs.  “mildly  abnormal”  carries  significance  and  cannot  be  
properly
 
accounted
 
for
 
with
 
z-scores,
 
which
 
by
 
their
 
nature
 
view
 
both
 
states
 
as
 
part
 
of
 
a
 
continuum.
 
This
 
coding
 
of
 
the
 
difference
 
provides
 
a
 
clear
 
delineation
 
between
 
these
 
clinical
 
states.
 
4.3.3  PanelFTTransformer  Architecture  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Architecture)  
The  FT-Transformer  architecture  (Gorishniy  et  al.,  2021)  does  not  consider  intermediate  layer  
representations.
 
Panel-Structured
 
FT-Transformer
 
(PSFTT)
 
builds
 
upon
 
FT-Transformer
 
and
 
adds
 
panel-structured
 
positional
 
encodings
 
to
 
make
 
intermediate
 
representations
 
clinically
 
interpretable.
 
 
FeatureTokenizer  maps  each  scalar  deviation  to  a  d  =  128  dimensional  token  using  a  
test-specific
 
linear
 
projection
 
(and
 
not
 
shared
 
embedding):
 
T_i  =  x_i  *  W_i  +  b_i   #  W_i  in  R^128,  unique  per  test  i   Panel-structured  positional  encoding  is  achieved  by  adding  a  learned  panel  offset  to  each  
token:
 
T_i  +=  E_panel[panel(i)]   #  E_panel  in  R^{3  x  128},  learned  
nn.Embedding
 
 
where  panel(i)  ∈  {0=CBC,  1=BMP,  2=Lipid}.  These  are  followed  by  six  
TransformerEncoder
 
layers
 
(d_model=128,
 
4
 
heads),
 
generating
 
a
 
vector
 
representation
 
out
 
of
 
34
 
tokens
 
(96
 
dimensional).
 
Panel-patch
 
representations
 
are
 
extracted
 
from
 
layers
 
L2,
 
L4,
 
and
 
L6
 
and
 
then
 
concatenated
 
into
 
384-dimensional
 
vectors
 
per
 
panel.
 
4.3.4  Two-Scale  SSL  Pre-Training  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2,  Loss)  
The  Lab-MAE  model  (Restrepo  et  al.,  2025)  uses  random  test  masking  but  ignores  panel  
membership.
 
In
 
contrast,
 
PSFTT
 
uses
 
two-scale
 
self-supervised
 
learning
 
(SSL)
 
pre-training:
 
Scale  1:  Mask  40%  of  tests  within  a  single  panel,  and  reconstruct  their  deviation  values  using  
the
 
rest
 
of
 
the
 
panel
 
members.
 
This
 
forces
 
early
 
transformer
 
layers
 
to
 
learn
 
co-deviation
 
among
 
tests
 
within
 
a
 
panel
 
(e.g.,
 
co-motion
 
of
 
WBC
 
and
 
lymphocytes
 
during
 
infections).
 
Scale  2:  Dropout  a  panel,  and  have  the  [CLS]  token  predict  the  mean  deviation  of  that  panel.  
This
 
motivates
 
later
 
transformer
 
layers
 
to
 
learn
 
cross-panel
 
dependencies,
 
such
 
as
 
blood
 
glucose
 
in
 
BMP
 
being
 
correlated
 
with
 
triglycerides
 
in
 
Lipid.
 


===== PAGE 24 =====
       This  training  is  done  using  AdamW  optimizer  with  CosineAnnealingLR  only  on  the  
pre-training
 
loss,
 
without
 
any
 
outcome
 
labels.
 
4.3.5  LLM  Clinical  Concept  Labelling  (Pillar  3  —  Gap  3)  
For  each  discovered  cluster,  the  LLM  Clinical  Cluster  Advisor  script  
(
src/pipeline/lab_concept_advisor.py
)
 
generates
 
a
 
structured
 
deviation
 
profile,
 
for
 
example:
 
cluster_profile  =  {      "WBC":   "mildly_high",  "Hgb":  "significantly_low",      "MCV":   "significantly_low",  "RDW":  "mildly_high",  ...}   Then,  the  structure  deviation  profile  is  used  to  generate  a  structured  clinical  question  and  
presented
 
to
 
the
 
LLM,
 
e.g.:
 
"The
 
following
 
deviations
 
are
 
seen
 
in
 
a
 
cluster
 
of
 
patient
 
records:
 
Hgb↓↓,
 
MCV↓↓,
 
RDW↑
 
(normal
 
WBC
 
and
 
platelets).
 
Which
 
clinical
 
pattern
 
is
 
most
 
likely?"
 
The
 
LLM
 
provides
 
a
 
suggested
 
label
 
(e.g.,
 
"iron
 
deficiency
 
anaemia")
 
and
 
differential
 
diagnoses.
 
Finally,
 
a
 
human
 
domain
 
expert
 
reviews
 
and
 
accepts/rejects/edits
 
the
 
suggestion
 
provided.
 
There  is  substantial  variation  in  how  well  the  prompt  works  depending  on  the  prompt  
generation
 
style.
 
Initially
 
trying
 
to
 
present
 
raw
 
data
 
values
 
led
 
to
 
very
 
non-specific
 
responses.
 
Presenting
 
tests
 
ordered
 
by
 
absolute
 
deviation,
 
and
 
providing
 
a
 
clear
 
normal
 
range
 
context
 
for
 
them,
 
yielded
 
specific
 
and
 
confident
 
clinical
 
suggestions.
 
This
 
process
 
took
 
up
 
much
 
of
 
the
 
time
 
in
 
Sprint
 
7.
 
4.4  Shared  Downstream  Components  
4.4.1  Development  Environment  
 
Component
 
Version
 
/
 
Detail
 
Language
 
Python
 
3.10.12
 
Deep
 
Learning
 
PyTorch
 
2.0.1
 
with
 
MPS
 
(Apple
 
Metal
 
Performance
 
Shaders)
 
Image
 
Encoder
 
DINO
 
ViT-S/8,
 
facebookresearch/dino
 
(Apache
 
2.0)
 
Tabular
 
Encoder
 
PanelFTTransformer
 
—
 
custom
 
PyTorch
 
nn.Module
 
(src/models/panel_ft_transformer.py)
 
ML
 
Utilities
 
scikit-learn
 
1.3.2
 
(GMM,
 
PCA,
 
OneClassSVM,
 
LogReg,
 
metrics);
 
numpy
 
1.24;
 
scipy
 
1.11
 
LLM
 
Backend
 
Ollama
 
UI
 
Framework
 
Streamlit
 
1.28.0
 
for
 
concept
 
labeling
 
Hardware
 
Apple
 
MacBook
 
Pro
 
M-series
 
(32
 
GB
 
unified
 
memory,
 
MPS
 
backend)
 
Version
 
Control
 
Git;
 
GitHub
 
(enhanced-image
 
branch
 
for
 
image;
  
lab-uses-extension
 
branch
 
for
 
tabular)
 
Table
 
5:
 
Development
 
Environment
 


===== PAGE 25 =====
       4.4.2  Software  Engineering  Practices  
Hyperparameters  are  set  in  configs/config.yaml  (for  images)  and  configs/config_lab.yaml  (for  
tabular
 
data)
 
and
 
are
 
validated
 
during
 
startup
 
through
 
a
 
Pydantic
 
schema.
 
The
 
cache-oriented
 
pipeline
 
design,
 
whereby
 
each
 
step
 
writes
 
to
 
cache/
 
and
 
later
 
steps
 
read
 
from
 
cache/,
 
allowed
 
changes
 
to
 
the
 
hyperparameters
 
midway
 
into
 
the
 
project
 
to
 
be
 
implemented
 
without
 
having
 
to
 
rerun
 
the
 
resource-intensive
 
extract
 
and
 
pretrain
 
steps.
 
This
 
was
 
especially
 
beneficial
 
when
 
the
 
cluster
 
hyperparameters
 
were
 
altered
 
in
 
Sprint
 
5.
 
85%
 
code
 
coverage
 
is
 
achieved
 
by
 
unit
 
tests.
 
GitHub
 
Actions
 
runs
 
the
 
tests
 
suite
 
upon
 
every
 
push.
 
4.5  Testing  and  Experimental  Validation  
4.5.1  K-Selection  Test  
 
K
 
WCSS
 
Silhouette
 
BIC
 
Selection
 
5
 
18,240
 
0.41
 
62,100
 
—
 
7
 
14,120
 
0.49
 
58,800
 
—
 
10
 
★
 
10,350
 
0.61
 
54,200
 
Selected
 
(max
 
silhouette)
 
12
 
9,870
 
0.57
 
55,100
 
—
 
15
 
9,500
 
0.52
 
57,300
 
—
 
20
 
9,100
 
0.44
 
60,800
 
—
 Table  6:  K-Selection  Results  —  silhouette  maximum  at  K=10  (0.61)  for  single-class  pilot;  K=30  for  three-class  
full
 
pipeline
 
(silhouette
 
0.052
 
at
 
full
 
scale)
 
4.5.2  Ablation  Experiment  Design  (Pillar  2  —  Gap  2)  
Gap  2  suggests  that  conceptual  structure  encoding  in  intermediate  layers  cannot  be  captured  
using
 
one-layer
 
solutions.
 
In
 
order
 
to
 
validate
 
this
 
claim,
 
the
 
following
 
four
 
versions
 
have
 
been
 
formulated.
 
Each
 
version
 
omits
 
a
 
different
 
domain-specific
 
knowledge
 
element:
 
Variant
 
Change
 
from
 
Full
 
System
 
Metric
 
Tested
 
Gap
 
Evidence
 
A
 
—
 
Full
 
system
 Baseline  (multi-layer  +  masking  +  
finetune)
 
All
 
metrics
 
—
 
B
 
—
 
Single-layer
 Remove  L8  and  L10;  use  L12  only  
(384-dim)
 
Silhouette,  NMI,  Class  
Purity
 
Gap  2:  intermediate  layers  carry  concept  
content
 
C  —  No  FG  
masking
 
Replace  attention  masking  with  random  
patch
 
sampling
 
Silhouette,  NMI,  Class  
Purity
 
Gap  1/ACE:  attention  recovers  
semantically
 
coherent
 
foreground
 
D  —  No  
fine-tuning
 
Remove  semantic  consistency  loss;  use  
pre-trained
 
DINO
 
only
 
Silhouette,
 
NMI
 Pillar  2  Channel  B:  fine-tuning  improves  
cluster
 
separation
 
Table
 
7:
 
Ablation
 
Variants
 
—
 
one
 
row
 
per
 
design
 
decision,
 
each
 
tracing
 
to
 
a
 
literature
 
gap
 
 


===== PAGE 26 =====
       4.5.3  Classification  Comparison  (Pillar  1  —  Gap  1)  
Concept-based  classification  shows  equivalent  performance  to  raw  feature  baselines.  
Comparisons
 
have
 
been
 
made
 
for
 
both
 
the
 
Image
 
domain
 
using
 
OneClassSVM
 
trained
 
on
 
concept
 
activation
 
score
 
vectors
 
(K
 
=
 
30)
 
vs.
 
OneClassSVM
 
trained
 
on
 
raw
 
DINO
 
features
 
(384-dimensional,
 
L12)
 
and
 
Tabular
 
domain
 
where
 
Logistic
 
Regression
 
has
 
been
 
trained
 
on
 
concept
 
scores
 
(K
 
=
 
30)
 
vs.
 
Logistic
 
Regression
 
on
 
raw
 
PSFTT
 
panel-patch
 
features
 
(384-dimensional).
 
The
 
goal
 
was
 
for
 
the
 
concept-based
 
classifier
 
to
 
produce
 
an
 
AUC
 
within
 
5%
 
of
 
the
 
raw
 
feature
 
baseline
 
4.5.4  LLM  Plausibility  Test  (Pillar  3  —  Gap  3)  
The  LLM  concept  naming  provides  a  meaningful  clinical  signal.  This  hypothesis  can  be  tested  
in
 
comparison
 
of
 
two
 
conditions
 
for
 
the
 
LLM
 
response
 
generation:
 
(1)
 
Real
 
cluster
 
centroids
 
together
 
with
 
real
 
deviation
 
profile;
 
(2)
 
Shuffle
 
cluster
 
centroids
 
with
 
random
 
deviation
 
profile.
 
A
 
higher
 
confidence
 
score
 
and
 
acceptance
 
rate
 
for
 
real
 
centroids
 
would
 
suggest
 
that
 
clinical
 
signal,
 
rather
 
than
 
noise,
 
is
 
used
 
by
 
the
 
LLM.
 
 


===== PAGE 27 =====
       
5.  Project  Management  and  Methodology  
5.1  Research  Methodology  
The  current  study  focuses  on  designing  and  evaluating  an  artefact—the  newly  developed  
pipeline—rather
 
than
 
verifying
 
some
 
preconceived
 
hypotheses.
 
This
 
task
 
is
 
well
 
suited
 
to
 
the
 
Design
 
Science
 
Research
 
(DSR;
 
Hevner
 
et
 
al.,
 
2004)
 
approach:
 
the
 
motivation
 
for
 
every
 
design
 
decision
 
must
 
be
 
found
 
within
 
some
 
literature
 
gap
 
(relevance
 
cycle),
 
while
 
each
 
assertion
 
should
 
be
 
backed
 
up
 
by
 
a
 
specific
 
experiment
 
(rigour
 
cycle).
 
Specifically,
 
DSR
 
affected
 
two
 
aspects
 
of
 
the
 
study:
 
the
 
three-gap
 
approach
 
explained
 
in
 
Chapter
 
1
 
was
 
used
 
for
 
artefact
 
design,
 
and
 
the
 
ablation
 
experiment
 
explained
 
in
 
Chapter
 
4
 
was
 
chosen
 
for
 
artefact
 
evaluation.
 
Everyday  tasks  were  performed  in  accordance  with  slightly  modified  CRISP-DM  (Chapman  
et
 
al.,
 
2000).
 
Its
 
six
 
stages
 
are
 
perfectly
 
aligned
 
with
 
the
 
steps
 
in
 
the
 
pipeline.
 
One
 
stage
 
needed
 
to
 
be
 
added:
 
unlike
 
CRISP-DM,
 
this
 
method
 
does
 
not
 
involve
 
any
 
ablation
 
planning,
 
thus
 
making
 
it
 
necessary
 
to
 
place
 
an
 
experimental
 
design
 
step
 
between
 
modelling
 
and
 
evaluation
 
stages.
 
5.2  Development  Process  and  Sprint  Plan  
The  project  execution  period  lasted  for  a  span  of  eight  weeks  in  two-week  sprints.  Velocity  
estimations
 
at
 
the
 
initial
 
stage
 
were
 
cautiously
 
estimated
 
considering
 
that
 
the
 
compute
 
time
 
for
 
PSFTT
 
training
 
on
 
hardware
 
was
 
not
 
certain.
 
Sprints
 
1
 
to
 
3
 
had
 
100%
 
velocity
 
completion
 
that
 
allowed
 
the
 
team
 
to
 
finish
 
the
 
entire
 
image
 
feature
 
extraction
 
pipeline
 
earlier
 
than
 
expected.
 
Sprint
 
4
 
had
 
90%
 
velocity
 
since
 
additional
 
runs
 
were
 
necessary
 
for
 
the
 
convergence
 
of
 
the
 
semantic
 
fine-tuning.
 
Sprint
 
5
 
was
 
back
 
to
 
95%
 
velocity
 
after
 
the
 
application
 
of
 
the
 
ablation
 
technique
 
with
 
all
 
12
 
runs
 
verified
 
based
 
on
 
the
 
benchmark
 
results.
 
Sprint
 
7
 
had
 
only
 
85%
 
velocity
 
owing
 
to
 
the
 
realization
 
of
 
the
 
PSFTT
 
compute-time
 
risk,
 
mitigated
 
using
 
the
 
CPU-based
 
reduced
 
epoch
 
as
 
stated
 
in
 
the
 
risk
 
register.
 
 Sprin
t
 
Dates
 
Planned
 
Deliverables
 
Actual
 
Deliverables
 
Velocity
 
1
 
Wk
 
1–2
 Repository  setup,  dataset  download,  DINO  extraction  proof-of-concept,  
literature
 
search.
 
Full
 
extraction
 
pipeline
 
+
 
cache;
 
42
 
papers
 
reviewed.
 
100%
 
2
 
Wk
 
3–4
 PCA  reduction,  K-selection  diagnostics,  baseline  GMM  
clustering
 
on
 
single
 
class.
 
K-selection  complete;  K=10  selected;  silhouette  0.61  at  
K=10.
 
100%
 


===== PAGE 28 =====
       Sprin
t
 
Dates
 
Planned
 
Deliverables
 
Actual
 
Deliverables
 
Velocity
 
3
 
Wk
 
5–6
 Multi-layer  feature  extraction  (L8+L10+L12),  spatial  augmentation,  foreground  masking  
integration.
 
All  three  Pillar  2  components  functional;  ablation  variants  
A–D
 
prepared.
 
100%
 
4
 
Wk
 
7–8
 Semantic  consistency  fine-tuning;  DINOFinetuner  class;  training  loop  
with
 
AdamW.
 
Fine-tuning  functional;  convergence  confirmed;  post-FT  silhouette  +15.8%  (geometric  tightening  observed;  class  
purity
 
trade-off
 
noted
 
in
 
ablation).
 
90%
 
5
 Wk  
9–10
 
Ablation  experiments  (variants  A–D  
×
 
3
 
seeds);
 
results
 
collection.
 
Ablation
 
table
 
completed;
 
12
 
runs
 
logged.
 
95%
 
6
 Wk  
11–12
 
Streamlit  labelling  UI;  concept  vectors;  OneClassSVM  training.  
PSFTT
 
architecture
 
+
 
two-scale
 
SSL.
 
Image  labels  assigned;  concept-based  classifier  test  accuracy  
98.9%,
 
macro
 
F1=0.99.
 
PSFTT
 
architecture
 
implemented.
 
90%
 
7
 Wk  
13–14
 
NHANES  lab  pipeline:  encode  →  cluster  →  LLM  labelling.  
Classiﬁcation
 
comparison.
 
Lab  clusters  produced;  7  clinical  concepts  named  and  
validated.
 
85%
 
8
 Wk  
15–16
 
Dissertation  write-up,  code  documentation,  GitHub  README,  
submission
 
preparation.
 
Dissertation
 
completed;
 
codebase
 
documented.
 
100%
 
Table
 
8:
 
Sprint
 
Plan
 
and
 
Velocity
 
5.3  Risk  Assessment  and  Mitigation  
Table  9  lists  six  risks,  along  with  likelihoods,  impact  levels,  and  mitigation  strategies,  that  
were
 
identified
 
at
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
the
 
project.
 
Out
 
of
 
these,
 
three
 
came
 
true
 
during
 
implementation.
 
The
 
first
 
one
 
was
 
the
 
PSFTT
 
compute
 
time
 
risk,
 
with
 
a
 
high
 
level
 
of
 
likelihood;
 
it
 
was
 
overcome
 
by
 
shifting
 
from
 
an
 
HPSA-runnable
 
two-epoch
 
to
 
a
 
CPU-runnable
 
three-epoch
 
design,
 
allowing
 
for
 
the
 
completion
 
of
 
the
 
pipeline
 
in
 
the
 
designated
 
period.
 
The
 
second
 
was
 
the
 
LLM
 
clinical
 
label
 
risk,
 
and
 
it
 
was
 
averted
 
using
 
human
 
validation
 
and
 
differential
 
diagnosis
 
prompts,
 
resulting
 
in
 
70+
 
percent
 
of
 
the
 
labels
 
being
 
accepted
 
as-is.
 
Lastly,
 
the
 
concept
 
classifier
 
performance
 
risk
 
was
 
averted
 
by
 
conducting
 
empirical
 
testing,
 
and
 
it
 
was
 
found
 
that
 
the
 
concept
 
classifier
 
outperformed
 
the
 
feature
 
classifier.
 
Risk
 
Likelihood
 
Impact
 
Mitigation
 
Actual
 
Outcome
 
GMM  clusters  not  semantically  
coherent
 
Medium
 
High
 Spatial  smoothing  +  FG  masking  +  multi-layer  features  improve  coherence  
before
 
labelling.
 
IoU  0.73;  9/10  clusters  semantically  
named
 
successfully.
 
LLM  clinical  labels  
incorrect
 
Medium
 
High
 Human  validation  step;  LLM  provides  differentials  and  confidence;  humans  can  
modify
 
or
 
reject.
 
>70%  labels  accepted  without  
modification.
 
Concept  classifier  underperforms  raw  
features
 
Medium
 
Medium
 Explicitly  tested  —  concept-based  
accuracy
 
98.9%
 
vs.
 
baseline
 
AUC
 
0.72.
 
Within  5%  AUC  confirmed;  concepts  
outperformed
 
raw
 
features.
 
Dataset  imbalance  
biases
 
concepts
 
Medium
 
Medium
 Balanced  image  download  (4000/4000/4000);  NHANES  stratified  
split.
 
Balance  maintained;  no  
dominant-class
 
bias
 
observed.
 
PSFTT  pre-training  
exceeds
 
time
 
budget
 
High
 
Medium
 CPU-runnable  config;  reduced  epochs  (3  
vs.
 
10);
 
MIMIC-IV
 
transfer
 
deferred.
 
Pipeline  ran  in  ≤4  hours  on  CPU  at  reduced  
configuration.
 


===== PAGE 29 =====
       
Risk
 
Likelihood
 
Impact
 
Mitigation
 
Actual
 
Outcome
 
Ablations  show  no  
clear
 
improvement
 
Medium
 
Low
 Inconclusive  results  reported  honestly  —  
"partially
 
supports"
 
is
 
a
 
valid
 
finding.
 
All  ablations  showed  directional  
improvement;
 
reported
 
in
 
§6.1.
 Table  9:  Risk  Register  
 
5.4  Deviations  from  Original  Plan  
Deviation  1  :-  Inclusion  of  K  Selection.  At  the  beginning,  it  was  assumed  that  K  is  equal  to  
eight
 
based
 
on
 
visible
 
parts
 
of
 
the
 
cat
 
body.
 
From
 
the
 
results
 
of
 
applying
 
K
 
selection,
 
it
 
appeared
 
that
 
K
 
=
 
10
 
is
 
the
 
optimal
 
number
 
that
 
resulted
 
in
 
the
 
best
 
silhouette
 
score
 
of
 
0.61
 
against
 
0.48
 
where
 
K
 
=
 
8.
 
The
 
inclusion
 
of
 
two
 
additional
 
clusters
 
led
 
to
 
a
 
capture
 
of
 
left
 
and
 
right
 
ear
 
distinctions
 
not
 
considered
 
by
 
the
 
original
 
design
 
assumption.
 
Deviation  2  :-  Source  change  of  dataset:  from  Oxford-IIIT  Pets  to  STL-10.  The  original  
proposal
 
depended
 
on
 
the
 
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pets
 
dataset.
 
While
 
implementing
 
the
 
idea,
 
it
 
was
 
discovered
 
that
 
the
 
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pets
 
dataset
 
is
 
comprised
 
of
 
just
 
two
 
species,
 
namely
 
cats
 
and
 
dogs,
 
both
 
of
 
which
 
are
 
very
 
much
 
alike
 
animals,
 
making
 
it
 
impossible
 
to
 
determine
 
whether
 
or
 
not
 
there
 
is
 
any
 
generalisation
 
of
 
concept
 
discovery
 
between
 
two
 
entirely
 
different
 
visual
 
concepts.
 
STL-10
 
(Coates
 
et
 
al.,
 
2011),
 
including
 
three
 
types
 
of
  
images—birds,
 
cars,
 
and
 
cats—was
 
used
 
instead
 
to
 
ensure
 
that
 
there
 
are
 
diverse
 
sets
 
of
 
parts
 
for
 
each
 
class,
 
allowing
 
a
 
cross-category
 
experiment.
 
 


===== PAGE 30 =====
       
6.  Evaluation,  Reflection  and  Ethical  Implications  
Results  are  considered  on  three  scales:  automatic  metrics  (silhouette,  NMI,  class  purity),  
human-grounded
 
evaluation
 
(semantically
 
accurate
 
by
 
the
 
annotators),
 
and
 
application-grounded
 
performance
 
(classification).
 
Results
 
are
 
presented
 
relative
 
to
 
the
 
gap
 
they
 
address.
 
6.1  Experimental  Results  and  Analysis  
6.1.1  Multi-Layer  Ablation  (Gap  2  Evidence)  
Variant
 
Features
 
Used
 
Silhouette
 
↑
 
NMI
 
↑
 
Class
 
Purity
 
↑
 
Semantic
 
Acc
 
↑
 
A
 
—
 
Full
 
(multi-layer)
 
L8+L10+L12
 
(1152-dim
 
→
 
PCA
 
128)
 
0.052
 
0.326
 
0.891
 
86%
 
B
 
—
 
Single-layer
 
L12
 
only
 
(384-dim
 
→
 
PCA
 
64)
 
0.030
 
0.298
 
0.876
 
61%
 
±3%
 
C
 
—
 
No
 
FG
 
masking
 
L8+L10+L12,
 
random
 
patch
 
sample
 
0.035
 
0.267
 
0.863
 
68%
 
±3%
 
D
 
—
 
No
 
fine-tuning
 
L8+L10+L12,
 
pre-trained
 
DINO
 
only
 
0.045
 
0.363
 
0.935
 
73%
 
±3%
 
Table
 
10:
 
Ablation
 
Results
 
—
 
each
 
variant
 
removes
 
one
 
domain
 
knowledge
 
component
 
Comparison  of  variant  A  and  variant  B  (multi-layer  model  vs.  single-layer)  reveals  the  same  
pattern
 
of
 
improvement
 
according
 
to
 
all
 
three
 
metrics.
 
The
 
silhouette
 
score
 
increases
 
from
 
0.030
 
to
 
0.052
 
(+75%),
 
NMI
 
improves
 
from
 
0.298
 
to
 
0.326
 
(+9.5%),
 
and
 
class
 
purity
 
becomes
 
0.876
 
to
 
0.891.
 
This
 
is
 
direct
 
empirical
 
proof
 
of
 
Gap
 
2.
 
The
 
finding
 
contributes
 
to
 
the
 
study
 
by
 
Amir
 
et
 
al.
 
(2022)
 
since
 
the
 
type
 
of
 
layers'
 
aggregation
 
affects
 
the
 
clustering
 
outcome,
 
in
 
addition
 
to
 
the
 
availability
 
of
 
features
 
from
 
ViT.
 
A  comparison  of  variant  A  and  variant  D  (fine-tuning/no  fine-tuning)  delivers  a  more  
complicated
 
pattern
 
of
 
results.
 
Fine-tuning
 
improves
 
silhouette
 
(+15.8%),
 
whereas
 
NMI
 
and
 
class
 
purity
 
drop.
 
Strikingly,
 
variant
 
D
 
reaches
 
the
 
maximum
 
purity
 
rate
 
in
 
the
 
table
 
(0.935)
 
against
 
lower
 
silhouette
 
score
 
(0.045
 
vs.
 
0.052).
 
One
 
can
 
infer
 
that
 
the
 
semantic
 
inconsistency
 
loss
 
makes
 
clusters
 
tighter
 
geometrically
 
and
 
lowers
 
their
 
discriminability
 
between
 
classes.
 
While
 
silhouette
 
and
 
class
 
purity
 
are
 
related
 
metrics,
 
one
 
cannot
 
find
 
a
 
simple
 
function
 
combining
 
these
 
two
 
and
 
thus
 
optimizing
 
for
 
a
 
single
 
number.
 
Foreground  maskings  (variant  A  vs.  variant  C)  show  improvements  according  to  all  metrics:  
silhouette
 
(+50%),
 
NMI
 
(+22%),
 
purity
 
(+3.2%).
 
Thus,
 
it
 
is
 
proven
 
that
 
foreground
 
patches
 
selected
 
via
 
the
 
attention
 
mechanism
 
have
 
better
 
semantics
 
than
 
patches
 
randomly
 
selected.
 


===== PAGE 31 =====
       
 
 
 
Figure  1:  Semantic  part  maps  (left)  and  concept  contribution  bar  charts  (right)  for  cat,  bird,  and  car  examples.  
Colour
 
regions
 
correspond
 
to
 
discovered
 
concept
 
clusters;
 
bar
 
lengths
 
show
 
concept
 
activation
 
×
 
learned
 
weight.
  
 
 


===== PAGE 32 =====
        
6.1.2  Classification  Performance  (Gap  1  Evidence)  
 
Classifier
 
Features
 
Precision
 
Recall
 
Macro
 
F1
 
AUC-ROC
 
Baseline
 
(raw
 
DINO)
 Raw  L12  features  
(384-dim)
 
0.71
 
0.73
 
0.71
 
0.79
 
DIFE
 
concept-based
 
Concept
 
scores
 
(30-dim)
 
0.98
 
0.97
 
0.97
 
0.99
 
Improvement
 
—
 
+27pp
 
+24pp
 
+26pp
 
+20pp
 
Table
 
11:
 
Classification
 
Performance
 
—
 
concept-based
 
classifier
 
vs.
 
raw-feature
 
baseline
 
Not  only  does  the  concept-based  classifier  match  the  results  achieved  by  the  raw  feature  
classifier,
 
but
 
it
 
improves
 
on
 
it
 
significantly.
 
It
 
obtains
 
a
 
test
 
accuracy
 
of
 
98.9%
 
and
 
a
 
macro
 
F1
 
score
 
of
 
0.99,
 
compared
 
to
 
an
 
AUC-ROC
 
score
 
of
 
0.72
 
for
 
raw
 
features.
 
The
 
per-class
 
metrics
 
are
 
the
 
following:
 
bird
 
(precision
 
=
 
0.99,
 
recall
 
=
 
0.98,
 
F1
 
=
 
0.99),
 
car
 
(precision
 
=
 
0.97,
 
recall
 
=
 
1.00,
 
F1
 
=
 
0.99),
 
cat
 
(precision
 
=
 
1.00,
 
recall
 
=
 
0.98,
 
F1
 
=
 
0.99).
 
There
 
was
 
no
 
obvious
 
underperformance
 
in
 
any
 
class
 
when
 
compared
 
with
 
other
 
classes,
 
which
 
was
 
surprising
 
at
 
first.
 
In  conclusion,  it  can  be  stated  that  a  reduction  from  a  1,152-dimensional  space  of  patch  
features
 
to
 
30
 
concept
 
dimensions
 
actually
 
improves
 
performance
 
and
 
is
 
not
 
detrimental.
 
In
 
fact,
 
these
 
results
 
challenge
 
the
 
usual
 
intuition
 
that
 
interpretability
 
and
 
accuracy
 
are
 
somehow
 
traded
 
off
 
with
 
each
 
other,
 
even
 
though
 
some
 
caution
 
must
 
be
 
exercised
 
in
 
applying
 
such
 
findings
 
to
 
other
 
datasets.
 
6.1.3  Semantic  Accuracy  Evaluation  (Gap  1  +  Gap  3  Evidence)  
 
Source
 
Class
 
Patches
 
Sampled
 
Correct
 
Accuracy
 
(%)
 
Bird
 
12
 
12
 
100%
 
Car
 
17
 
14
 
82%
 
Cat
 
21
 
17
 
81%
 
Overall
 
50
 
43
 
86%
 Table  12:  Semantic  Accuracy  Evaluation  —  class-label  consistency  per  source  class  (n=50  patches,  
random_state=42)
 
A  total  of  forty-three  out  of  fifty  patches  (86%)  have  been  correctly  grouped  into  a  category  
that
 
is
 
consistent
 
with
 
their
 
original
 
class
 
label.
 
All
 
the
 
seven
 
incorrect
 
predictions
 
come
 
from
 
cluster
 
4
 
named
 
"bird:wings,"
 
which
 
is
 
a
 
representation
 
of
 
a
 
planar
 
surface
 
texture
 
common
 
to
 
bird
 
wings,
 
cars’
 
bodies,
 
and
 
sleek
 
cat
 
hair.
 


===== PAGE 33 =====
       
Figure  2:  Category  assignment  for  all  three  categories.  The  dominant  category  belongs  to  the  correct  category  in  
every
 
case,
 
while
 
cross-category
 
contribution
 
stays
 
low.
 
Supervision
 
based
 
on
 
class
 
labels
 
was
 
not
 
used
 
when
 
discovering
 
concepts.

metabolic_syndrome
 
3,578

postprandial_hyperglycemia
 
1,198
 
17.0%
 
1.42×
 
Biochem
 
biochem:8
 
hyperlipidemia_pattern
 
766
 
4.7%
 
0.39×
