lipid:6
 
mild_hyperglycemia
 
1,131
 
19.1%
 
1.59×
 
(max)
 
Lipid
 
lipid:9
 
metabolic_syndrome
 
2,740
 
18.5%
 
1.54×
 
Lipid
 
lipid:7
 
hyperlipidemia_pattern
 
2,588
 
5.4%
 
0.45×
 
Table
 
13:
 
Tabular
 
Domain
 
—
 
Clinical
 
Concept
 
Clusters
 
from
 
NHANES
 
(PSFTT
 
Pipeline)
 
Thirty  clinical  concept  clusters  were  found,  ten  for  each  panel,  with  clearly  defined  names  and  
high
 
validation,
 
accepting
 
more
 
than
 
90%
 
of
 
cases.
 
It
 
is
 
noteworthy
 
that
 
the
 
same
 
clinical
 
concept
 
is
 
independently
 
found
 
among
 
the
 
panels
 
for
 
the
 
CBC,
 
Biochem,
 
and
 
Lipid
 
feature
 
sets,
 
each
 
having
 
enrichment
 
ratios
 
of
 
diabetes
 
prevalence
 
between
 
1.4-1.6
 
times
 
the
 
baseline
 
of
 
12%.
 
Maximum
 
enrichment
 
is
 
achieved
 
in
 
the
 
“mild_hyperglycemia”
 
class
 
in
 
the
 
Lipid
 
feature
 
set,
 
at
 
1.59×.
 
The
 
hypercholesterolemia
 
and
 
hyperlipidemia_pattern
 
clusters
 
have
 
diabetes
 
ratios
 
lower
 
than
 
the
 
baseline
 
(0.30-0.45×).
 
The  three-class  concept-based  classifier  reaches  an  accuracy  of  50.9%,  surpassing  the  random  
baseline
 
of
 
33.3%.
 
While
 
relatively
 
low
 
on
 
its
 
own,
 
this
 
accuracy
 
should
 
be
 
taken
 
into
 
account
 
when
 
considering
 
that
 
no
 
labels
 
were
 
available
 
during
 
the
 
concept
 
discovery
 
process.
 


===== PAGE 34 =====
       6.2  Objectives  vs.  Outcomes  
All  four  objectives  have  been  accomplished,  with  Objective  4  exceeding  expectations:  while  
the
 
concept-based
 
classifier
 
matched
 
the
 
baseline
 
classifier
 
on
 
accuracy,
 
it
 
surpassed
 
it
 
by
 
achieving
 
98.9%
 
test
 
accuracy
 
and
 
a
 
macro
 
F1
 
of
 
0.99
 
compared
 
to
 
the
 
baseline
 
classifier’s
 
AUC
 
of
 
0.72.
 
It
 
is
 
remarkable
 
to
 
note
 
that
 
this
 
objective
 
directly
 
contradicts
 
the
 
widely-held
 
assumption
 
that
 
interpretability
 
comes
 
at
 
the
 
cost
 
of
 
accuracy.
 
On
 
the
 
other
 
hand,
 
Objective
 
3
 
can
 
be
 
considered
 
to
 
have
 
been
 
largely
 
met.
 
However,
 
since
 
there
 
was
 
no
 
out-of-distribution
 
test
 
conducted
 
using
 
the
 
MIMIC-IV
 
dataset,
 
it
 
can
 
be
 
considered
 
only
 
partially
 
fulfilled
 
as
 
it
 
is
 
beyond
 
the
 
purview
 
of
 
this
 
study
 
(as
 
mentioned
 
in
 
Section
 
5.4).
 
Objective
 
Success
 
Criterion
 
Achieved?
 
Evidence
 
Obj.  1:  Domain-informed  
image
 
pipeline
 
Runnable  codebase;  3  domain  knowledge  components;  no  
ground-truth
 
labels
 
Fully
 
achieved
 GitHub  enhanced-image  branch;  multi-layer  +  masking  +  finetune  all  
functional
 
Obj.
 
2:
 
Ablation
 
experiments
 Variants  A–D  with  silhouette,  
NMI,
 
purity,
 
AUC-ROC
 
Fully
 
achieved
 Table  10;  Variant  A  best  silhouette;  nuanced  trade-off  in  fine-tuning  step  
documented
 
Obj.  3:  Tabular  PSFTT  
pipeline
 
+
 
LLM
 
labelling
 
Lab  pipeline  running;  ≥5  clinical  concepts  named;  >70%  LLM  
acceptance
 
rate
 
Substantially  
achieved
 
Table
 
13;
 
7
 
concepts;
 
>70%
 
acceptance
 
Obj.  4:  Concept  accuracy  vs.  
baseline
 
AUC  gap  ≤5%  between  concept-based  and  raw-feature  
classifiers
 
Exceeded:  concepts  
outperform
 
raw
 
Test  accuracy  98.9%,  macro  F1=0.99  
(concept-based)
 
vs.
 
AUC
 
0.72
 
(raw)
 
Table
 
14:
 
Objectives
 
vs.
 
Outcomes
 
Mapping
 
6.3  Comparison  with  Alternative  Approaches  
Concept  attributions  for  one  sample  per  class  are  illustrated  in  Figure  1.  Ground-truth  class  
concepts
 
receive
 
by
 
far
 
the
 
largest
 
attribution,
 
whereas
 
cross-class
 
concept
 
contribution
 
stays
 
effectively
 
zero
 
throughout.
 
The
 
distribution
 
of
 
concept
 
assignments
 
across
 
source
 
classes
 
is
 
shown
 
in
 
Figure
 
2.
 
Relative  to  Amir  et  al.  (2022)’s  direct  baseline  -  a  single-layer  k-means  clustering  algorithm  
run
 
on
 
DINO
 
embeddings
 
-
 
the
 
hierarchical
 
GMM,
 
augmented
 
with
 
domain-specific
 
masking,
 
provides
 
a
 
significant
 
boost
 
in
 
performance:
 
the
 
silhouette
 
score
 
jumps
 
75%,
 
class
 
purity
 
is
 
enhanced
 
by
 
21%,
 
and
 
classifier
 
accuracy
 
(AUC)
 
increases
 
from
 
0.72
 
to
 
0.989.
 
With  regards  to  the  related  literature:  TCAV  needs  200  labeled  samples  per  concept  despite  
having
 
a
 
supervised
 
backbone.
 
DIFE
 
delivers
 
concept-level
 
interpretability
 
with
 
0
 
concept
 
labels
 
and
 
a
 
self-supervised
 
backbone.
 
In
 
contrast,
 
concept-based
 
models
 
(CBMs)
 
produce
 
fine-grained
 
interpretations
 
with
 
an
 
F1-score
 
of
 
0.78–0.83
 
under
 
full
 
supervision.
 
DIFE
 


===== PAGE 35 =====
       achieves  F1  =  0.97  in  an  unsupervised  setting.  STEGO  produces  a  mean  IoU  score  of  0.39  on  
Oxford-IIIT
 
Pets
 
dataset
 
without
 
supervision,
 
but
 
with
 
semantic
 
accuracy
 
of
 
86%,
 
DIFE
 
shows
 
comparable
 
or
 
even
 
better
 
concept
 
coherence.
 
There  is  currently  no  comparison  available  against  CBMs  with  full  concept  supervision  
training
 
on
 
STL-10,
 
as
 
this
 
experiment
 
was
 
outside
 
the
 
scope
 
of
 
the
 
current
 
work.
 
However,
 
such
 
a
 
comparison
 
would
 
clearly
 
show
 
whether
 
“interpretable
 
without
 
supervision”
 
is
 
on
 
par
 
with
 
“interpretable
 
with
 
supervision”
 
for
 
this
 
problem
 
setting.
 
6.4  Critical  Appraisal  —  What  Worked  and  What  Did  Not  
Regarding  the  ablation  structure,  I  feel  most  confident  about  the  ablations  themselves  because  
of
 
the
 
way
 
that
 
each
 
was
 
constructed
 
to
 
vary
 
only
 
one
 
design
 
choice
 
at
 
a
 
time,
 
with
 
explicit
 
connection
 
to
 
an
 
open
 
literature
 
gap.
 
The
 
directional
 
agreement
 
seen
 
in
 
three
 
of
 
the
 
four
 
ablation
 
variants
 
regarding
 
the
 
relationship
 
between
 
silhouette
 
score
 
and
 
class
 
purity
 
was
 
heartening.
 
The
 
fact
 
that
 
variant
 
D
 
exhibited
 
a
 
different
 
behavior
 
gave
 
us
 
information
 
beyond
 
what
 
a
 
monotonic
 
pattern
 
could,
 
demonstrating
 
that
 
fine-tuning
 
changes
 
the
 
allocation
 
of
 
class-discriminative
 
material
 
in
 
a
 
non-geometric
 
fashion.
 
The  tabular  pipeline  is  the  aspect  that  I  am  least  satisfied  with.  PSFTT  does  a  good  job  and  
gives
 
plausible
 
clustering
 
according
 
to
 
clinical
 
expectations,
 
but
 
there
 
is
 
no
 
out-of-distribution
 
test
 
of
 
the
 
method.
 
The
 
idea
 
was
 
to
 
run
 
a
 
transfer
 
test
 
on
 
the
 
MIMIC-IV
 
data,
 
i.e.,
 
identify
 
concepts
 
on
 
the
 
NHANES
 
data
 
and
 
then
 
use
 
those
 
clusters
 
on
 
the
 
MIMIC-IV
 
ICU
 
dataset.
 
This
 
was
 
intended
 
to
 
be
 
a
 
truly
 
strong
 
result,
 
although
 
the
 
lack
 
thereof
 
has
 
been
 
a
 
limitation
 
of
 
the
 
work
 
for
 
which
 
I
 
recognize
 
responsibility
 
since
 
deciding
 
to
 
delay
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
the
 
MIMIC-IV
 
data.
 
The  semantic  accuracy  assessment  has  a  known  ceiling.  The  automated  check  uses  the  image  
class
 
labels
 
as
 
a
 
proxy
 
for
 
the
 
ground
 
truth
 
and
 
is
 
thus
 
unable
 
to
 
pick
 
up
 
on
 
within-class
 
mistakes
 
(e.g.,
 
two
 
types
 
of
 
cat
 
texture
 
in
 
a
 
single
 
cluster).
 
The
 
gold
 
standard
 
test
 
would
 
involve
 
manually
 
labeling
 
a
 
few
 
images
 
with
 
pixel-level
 
part
 
annotations.
 
Streamlit  labeling  tool  did  not  go  through  usability  testing.  It  is  unknown  whether  the  process  
of
 
human
 
annotation
 
is
 
efficient
 
or
 
cognitively
 
heavy.
 
This
 
question
 
may
 
be
 
answered
 
in
 
further
 
research
 
through
 
user
 
testing.
 
The  LLM  labeling  was  unexpectedly  successful  when  using  the  right  prompting  method.  The  
first
 
mistake
 
made
 
was
 
ambiguous
 
answers
 
when
 
using
 
raw
 
numerical
 
input.
 
It
 
is
 
something
 


===== PAGE 36 =====
       that  taught  me  how  to  give  clinical  data  to  a  language  model  in  such  a  way  that  may  prove  
useful
 
for
 
future
 
work.
 
It
 
is
 
currently
 
implemented
 
in
 
src/pipeline/lab_concept_advisor.py.
 
6.5  Ethical,  Legal  and  Commercial  Implications  
The  fundamental  ethical  impetus  behind  the  project  is  the  constructive  one;  auditing  clinical  
AI
 
improves
 
safety
 
meaningfully.
 
The
 
format
 
used
 
for
 
providing
 
concept
 
explanations
 
complies
 
with
 
the
 
intent
 
of
 
GDPR
 
Article
 
22
 
and
 
satisfies
 
the
 
demands
 
of
 
the
 
EU
 
AI
 
Act
 
in
 
regards
 
to
 
human
 
oversight
 
of
 
HRAI
 
systems.
 
Data  ethics:  all  the  datasets  utilized  here  are  publicly  shareable  without  any  individual  
identifiers.
 
No
 
protected
 
health
 
information
 
was
 
collected
 
or
 
transmitted
 
throughout
 
the
 
work
 
process.
 
By
 
default,
 
LLM
 
Advisor
 
runs
 
on
 
the
 
local
 
Ollama
 
instance;
 
use
 
of
 
the
 
Claude
 
API
 
service
 
is
 
voluntary
 
and
 
adheres
 
to
 
Anthropic’s
 
privacy
 
policies.
 
There  is  an  evident  bias  risk  that  should  be  taken  into  consideration:  unsupervised  concept  
discovery
 
mirrors
 
the
 
composition
 
of
 
the
 
corpus
 
used
 
for
 
model
 
pretraining.
 
Underrepresented
 
communities
 
of
 
patients
 
may
 
not
 
receive
 
proper
 
representation
 
in
 
the
 
discovered
 
concepts
 
space.
 
Human
 
validation
 
is
 
the
 
main
 
mitigation
 
measure,
 
although
 
it
 
is
 
not
 
foolproof.
 
In
 
particular,
 
an
 
expert
 
working
 
rapidly
 
could
 
miss
 
out
 
on
 
a
 
concept
 
that
 
is
 
consistently
 
missing
 
among
 
certain
 
demographics
 
of
 
interest.
 
A
 
quantitative
 
check
 
based
 
on
 
enrichment
 
analysis
 
(see
 
Table
 
13)
 
is
 
another
 
way
 
of
 
verifying
 
whether
 
such
 
issues
 
arise.
 
Commercial  considerations:  the  modular  nature  of  the  system  allows  its  deployment  in  other  
contexts,
 
e.g.,
 
vehicle
 
inspection,
 
manufacturing
 
quality
 
control,
 
and
 
radiology
 
report
 
generation.
 
Domain
 
experts
 
require
 
no
 
understanding
 
of
 
machine
 
learning
 
algorithms
 
to
 
properly
 
label
 
images:
 
a
 
half-hour
 
naming
 
procedure
 
is
 
sufficient.
 
6.6  Personal  Reflection  
The  most  interesting  finding  was  not  one  of  the  primary  metrics.  In  particular,  the  ablation  test  
showed
 
that
 
single-layer
 
features
 
(Variant
 
B)
 
performed
 
21%
 
worse
 
semantically
 
than
 
multi-layer
 
features
 
(Variant
 
A).
 
I
 
performed
 
that
 
experiment
 
thrice
 
using
 
different
 
seeds
 
to
 
confirm
 
the
 
discrepancy,
 
but
 
the
 
results
 
were
 
consistent
 
every
 
time.
 
My
 
initial
 
expectation
 
was
 
that
 
the
 
information
 
contained
 
in
 
the
 
last
 
DINO
 
block
 
should
 
dominate—in
 
all
 
of
 
the
 
attention-map
 
analysis
 
done
 
on
 
DINO
 
models,
 
authors
 
stress
 
that
 
importance.
 
Clearly,
 
my
 
expectation
 
turned
 
out
 
to
 
be
 
unfounded.
 


===== PAGE 37 =====
       I  found  the  tabular  learning  aspect  of  the  project  to  be  significantly  harder  than  initially  
expected.
 
Moreover,
 
this
 
issue
 
arose
 
not
 
as
 
predicted
 
by
 
me.
 
While
 
designing
 
the
 
PSFTT
 
architecture
 
proved
 
less
 
difficult
 
after
 
resolving
 
the
 
issue
 
of
 
panel
 
positional
 
encoding
 
design,
 
the
 
main
 
problem
 
was
 
the
 
need
 
to
 
receive
 
confident
 
medical
 
advice
 
in
 
response
 
from
 
the
 
LLM
 
instead
 
of
 
vague
 
predictions.
 
Prompt
 
engineering
 
took
 
up
 
quite
 
a
 
bit
 
of
 
time
 
in
 
Sprint
 
7
 
but
 
does
 
not
 
manifest
 
in
 
any
 
way
 
in
 
the
 
final
 
results
 
table.
 
As  to  the  management  aspect:  the  risk  register  was  more  helpful  than  expected.  Before  
conducting
 
the
 
benchmarks,
 
the
 
PSFTT
 
compute-time
 
risk
 
was
 
assessed
 
as
 
"High
 
likelihood."
 
The
 
fact
 
that
 
this
 
risk
 
actually
 
occurred
 
could
 
be
 
dealt
 
with
 
through
 
already
 
established
 
mitigating
 
actions,
 
namely
 
CPU-runnable
 
design,
 
reduction
 
in
 
number
 
of
 
epochs,
 
and
 
deferral
 
of
 
MIMIC-IV
 
experiments.
 
The
 
takeaway
 
here
 
for
 
a
 
future
 
similar
 
project
 
is
 
to
 
develop
 
mitigating
 
actions
 
in
 
advance.
 
If  I  had  the  opportunity  to  start  the  project  again,  I  would  file  an  MIMIC-IV  data  access  
request
 
in
 
Sprint
 
1.
 
Data
 
acquisition
 
is
 
the
 
only
 
dependency
 
in
 
the
 
project
 
workflow
 
that
 
cannot
 
be
 
expedited
 
in
 
any
 
way,
 
no
 
matter
 
how
 
efficient
 
the
 
remaining
 
parts
 
may
 
become.
 
Moreover,
 
an
 
additional
 
transfer
 
experiment
 
would
 
further
 
strengthen
 
the
 
argument
 
regarding
 
practical
 
relevance
 
of
 
my
 
results.
 


===== PAGE 38 =====
       
7.  Conclusion  and  Future  Work  
7.1  Summary  of  Contributions  
The  key  research  question  is  whether  the  model  can  self-supervise  to  generate  its  own  
concepts,
 
learn
 
labels
 
that
 
have
 
been
 
assigned
 
by
 
domain
 
experts,
 
and
 
then
 
use
 
the
 
same
 
labels
 
to
 
explain
 
its
 
reasoning
 
mechanism
 
without
 
any
 
supervision
 
during
 
training.
 
Results
 
show
 
that
 
this
 
is
 
possible,
 
at
 
least
 
in
 
the
 
case
 
of
 
the
 
two
 
domains
 
explored
 
in
 
the
 
current
 
study.
 
To
 
what
 
extent
 
this
 
finding
 
applies
 
elsewhere
 
will
 
be
 
addressed
 
in
 
future
 
research.
 
This  paper  solves  three  problems  that  were  left  open  by  previous  research.  Problem  1  is  
concerned
 
with
 
the
 
requirement
 
of
 
labeled
 
concepts
 
before
 
training,
 
since
 
Gaussian
 
Mixture
 
Model
 
(GMM)
 
clustering
 
generates
 
significant
 
concepts
 
after
 
training;
 
moreover,
 
ablation
 
studies
 
show
 
semantic
 
consistency
 
and
 
classification
 
accuracy
 
at
 
86%
 
and
 
98.9%,
 
respectively.
 
Problem
 
2
 
is
 
the
 
lack
 
of
 
exploration
 
of
 
intermediate
 
layers
 
in
 
the
 
transformers:
 
multi-layer
 
concatenation
 
results
 
in
 
a
 
75%
 
improvement
 
in
 
silhouette
 
score
 
compared
 
to
 
single-layer
 
models.
 
Problem
 
3
 
is
 
related
 
to
 
the
 
use
 
of
 
concept
 
labels
 
generated
 
from
 
large
 
language
 
models
 
(LLMs)
 
in
 
analyzing
 
numerical
 
clinical
 
data:
 
LLM
 
Clinical
 
Advisor
 
achieves
 
more
 
than
 
90%
 
acceptance
 
rate;
 
besides,
 
the
 
clinical
 
consistency
 
in
 
terms
 
of
 
disease
 
enrichment
 
ratio
 
of
 
1.4×–1.6×
 
is
 
obtained
 
across
 
all
 
three
 
clinical
 
panels.
 
To  address  the  stated  problems,  the  present  study  proposes  five  concrete  innovations:  (1)  
DIFE—an
 
entirely
 
self-supervised
 
architecture
 
including
 
nine
 
steps
 
of
 
training
 
that
 
require
 
no
 
ground
 
truth
 
annotations
 
and
 
released
 
on
 
GitHub;
 
(2)
 
PanelFTTransformer
 
with
 
panel-position
 
encoding
 
and
 
dual-scale
 
self-supervised
 
pre-training;
 
first
 
attempt
 
to
 
explore
 
intermediate
 
transformer
 
representations
 
as
 
concept
 
representations;
 
(3)
 
LLM
 
Clinical
 
Cluster
 
Advisor—extension
 
of
 
ClusterLLM
 
into
 
numeric-clinical
 
data;
 
(4)
 
experimental
 
verification
 
that
 
cluster-proportion-based
 
scoring
 
is
 
better
 
than
 
cosine
 
similarity
 
as
 
concept
 
activation
 
method;
 
(5)
 
a
 
new
 
phenomenon
 
found
 
that
 
semantic
 
consistency
 
fine-tuning
 
improves
 
cluster
 
geometry
 
and
 
re-distributes
 
class
 
discrimination.
 
7.2  Future  Work  
External  validation  using  MIMIC-IV  is  of  utmost  importance.  External  validation  in  terms  of  
translating
 
laboratory
 
concepts
 
learned
 
using
 
NHANES
 
to
 
the
 
records
 
of
 
ICU
 
patients
 
on
 
MIMIC-IV
 
would
 
be
 
a
 
strong
 
signal
 
of
 
generalization.
 
If
 
laboratory
 
concepts
 
learned
 
using
 
NHANES
 
are
 
able
 
to
 
accurately
 
predict
 
sepsis
 
and
 
AKI
 
status
 
for
 
records
 
on
 
MIMIC-IV
 
with
 


===== PAGE 39 =====
       an  AUC  score  greater  than  0.6,  then  it  could  be  used  to  substantiate  the  claims  made  in  this  
dissertation
 
paper
 
as
 
out-of-distribution
 
validation.
 
However,
 
the
 
lack
 
of
 
external
 
validation
 
would
 
mean
 
all
 
table-based
 
results
 
are
 
still
 
in-distribution.
 
Completing  full  pre-training  using  PSFTT  for  10  epochs  on  GPU  would  ensure  
methodologically
 
similar
 
reproducibility
 
as
 
the
 
image-domain
 
results
 
while
 
ensuring
 
consistency
 
with
 
previous
 
experiments.
 
The
 
current
 
results
 
have
 
been
 
based
 
on
 
limited
 
pre-training
 
done
 
on
 
CPU
 
using
 
reduced
 
epochs.
 
Multi-domain  generalization  test  using  birds  dataset  and  Stanford  cars  would  help  us  
understand
 
if
 
the
 
improved
 
performance
 
of
 
pipeline
 
models
 
for
 
image
 
classification
 
is
 
specific
 
to
 
a
 
particular
 
task
 
or
 
generalization
 
across
 
various
 
other
 
visual
 
recognition
 
tasks.
 
A  hierarchical  Bayesian  mixture  model  of  concepts  can  enable  the  identification  of  part-of  
relationships
 
like
 
"face"
 
being
 
a
 
component
 
of
 
"head".
 
Thus,
 
such
 
a
 
concept
 
learning
 
process
 
would
 
be
 
helpful
 
for
 
identifying
 
anatomical
 
features
 
in
 
clinical
 
imaging.
 
Concept-level  fairness  assessment  would  be  an  important  aspect  to  investigate  as  this  can  
reveal
 
whether
 
prediction
 
bias
 
arises
 
due
 
to
 
the
 
influence
 
of
 
protected
 
demographic
 
concepts.
 
This
 
advantage
 
cannot
 
be
 
achieved
 
using
 
raw
 
feature-based
 
classifiers.
 
 


===== PAGE 40 =====

data’

in

by

feature
 
correspondences’,

Quarterly,

beyond

with

vectors

learning
 
of

(CHIL).

objects

no

machine

and

Appendices  
Appendix  A:  Project  Specification  Reference  
The  project  specification  “04_Interpretable_Space.pdf”  is  provided  by  the  supervisor  for  this  
dissertation.
 
The
 
three
 
gaps
 
and
 
four
 
objectives
 
extracted
 
from
 
the
 
specification
 
are
 
reproduced
 
in
 
Sections
 
1.2
 
and
 
1.5
 
respectively.
 
Appendix  B:  GitHub  Repository  
Pipeline  URL:   
https://github.com/bhoopeshsingh/unsupervised-parts-discovery [branch:  main]  
Repository  contains:  full  Python  source  for  all  pipeline  stages;  configs/config.yaml  and  
configs/config_lab.yaml;
 
unit
 
tests
 
in
 
tests/;
 
README
 
with
 
quickstart
 
commands;
 
requirements.txt.
